questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText:
A student studying geography observed the
following about two towns:
Town A experienced a sudden drop in temperature,
rainfall and strong winds over two days.
Town B has consistently received moderate
rainfall and has mild temperatures for decades.
Based on this observation, which of the following
inferences is most appropriate?

options.a:
Town A is demonstrating a feature of climate,
while Town B reflects a weather condition.
options.b:
Both Town A and Town B are experiencing
weather phenomena.
options.c:
Town A is demonstrating a weather event,
while Town B reflects a climatic pattern.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Temperature, pressure, wind, humidity and precipitation, interact with each other. They influence the atmospheric conditions like the direction and velocity of wind, amount of insolation, cloud-cover and the amount of precipitation. These are known as the elements of both weather and climate.
● **Weather** is the atmospheric condition of **any place for a short period of time** with respect to one or more elements such as temperature, pressure, wind, humidity, precipitation, sunshine, cloud cover etc.
● Town **A’s description fits the short-term atmospheric condition, which is weather,** while **Town B shows a long-term, averaged pattern, characteristic of climate.**
● Following is the difference between weather and climate:
**Weather** **Climate**
● Weather is the study of atmospheric conditions for a short duration of a limited area.
● Climate is the study of the average weather conditions observed over a long period of time for a larger area.
● Weather is influenced by anyone of its predominant elements i.e., temperature or humidity.
● Climate is the collective effect of all its elements.
● The weather changes very often.
● It is more or less permanent.
● It is experienced over small areas of a country.
● It is experienced over a large area of the continent.
● A place can experience different types of weather conditions in a year.
● A place can experience only one type of climate.
**Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the Big Bang Theory:
I. - The universe began from an extremely hot
and dense state known as a singularity.
II. 	 Matter in the universe started forming within
the first three minutes after the Big Bang.
III. 	 The universe became transparent after
about 3 million years, when the temperature
dropped to 4,500 K.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A large number of hypotheses were put forth by different philosophers and scientists regarding the origin of the earth. The most popular argument regarding the **origin of the universe** **is the Big Bang Theory**. It is also called the expanding universe hypothesis. **Edwin Hubble,** in 1920, provided evidence that the **universe is expanding.** As time passes, galaxies move further and further apart.
● The Big Bang Theory considers the following stages in the development of the universe:
○ In the beginning, **all matter forming the universe** existed in one place in the form of a **“tiny ball”** **(singular atom)** with an unimaginably small volume, infinite temperature and infinite density. **So, statement** **I is correct.** ○ At the Big Bang the “tiny ball” exploded violently. This led to a huge expansion. It is now generally accepted that the event of the **big bang took place 13.7 billion years before the present**. The expansion continues even to the present day. As it grew, some energy was converted into matter. There was particularly rapid expansion within fractions of a second after the bang. Thereafter, the expansion has slowed down. Within the first **three minutes from the Big** **Bang event**, **the first atom began to form. So, statement II is correct.** ○ **Within 300,000 years from the Big Bang**, temperature dropped to **4,500K (Kelvin)** and gave rise to atomic matter. The **universe became transparent. So, statement III is** **correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Origin, Evolution, Geological Time Scale
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** Hydrogen and helium were stripped away from the earth during the early stage of atmospheric evolution.
**Statement II:** Earth had low gravity and was exposed to intense solar activity. **Statement III:** Hydrogen and helium are relatively light gases.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain Statement I
options.b: Both Statement II and Statement III are correct, but only one of them explains Statement I
options.c: Only one of the Statements II and III is correct and that explains Statement I
options.d: Neither Statement II nor Statement III is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The atmosphere did not exist in its current life-sustaining form when the Earth was formed about **4.6** **billion years ago**. Instead, it underwent a series of **transformational stages** due to geological, chemical, and biological processes. These changes were driven by factors like **solar radiation, volcanic** **activity and biological evolution**, particularly the emergence of **photosynthetic organisms**.
● The **first stage of atmospheric evolution** was marked by the loss of the **primordial atmosphere,**
which consisted **mainly of hydrogen and helium. The atmosphere was stripped away by solar** **winds,** not only from Earth but also from other terrestrial planets. This occurred because the **early** **Earth had low gravity and was exposed to intense solar activity,** which prevented it from retaining **these light gases.** **So, statements I, II and III are correct.**
● **The second stage began as Earth cooled.** Gases like water vapour, carbon dioxide, nitrogen, and methane **were released from the Earth's interior through a process called degassing,** which was largely driven by continuous volcanic eruptions**. This activity led to the formation of the** **secondary atmosphere,** richer in heavier gases but still lacking free oxygen. This composition laid the foundation for the later development of oceans and life.
● **The third stage involved a significant transformation brought about by the emergence of** **photosynthetic organisms in Earth's oceans**. Around 2,500 to 3,000 million years ago, life began producing oxygen through photosynthesis. Over time, the oceans became saturated with oxygen, and around 2,000 million years ago, oxygen started to accumulate in the atmosphere, leading to the development of the present oxygen-rich atmosphere. **So, both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain Statement I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Insurance and Pension
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Optimal distance from the Sun
II. 	 Appropriate mass and size
III. 	 Earth’s magnetic field
How many of the above factors contribute for making Earth a habitable planet?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A habitable planet is one that can sustain life for a significant period; based on our solar system, life requires **liquid water, energy and nutrients**. A **"habitable zone"** is the region around a star where planets can receive the perfect amount of heat to maintain liquid water on their surfaces. Because Earth is in the habitable zone of the Sun, this arrangement has informed our understanding of habitability. The Earth is often referred to as a **"Goldilocks planet"**, meaning it has **just the right conditions** to support life, not too hot, not too cold.
● **Earth remains at an optimal distance from the Sun** in an almost circular orbit. This ensures that it **receives** just the right amount of **solar energy, keeping surface temperatures** within a range suitable for the origin and evolution of life. **So, point I is correct.**
● **Earth has a suitable mass and radius,** which allows it to exert a  **gravitational force strong** **enough to retain the atmosphere,** a key requirement for sustaining life**. So, point II is correct.**
● Earth’s magnetic field plays a protective role. It **shields the surface from harmful high-energy solar** **radiation,** particularly solar wind and cosmic rays, by deflecting charged particles. Without this magnetic shield, Earth’s atmosphere could have been gradually stripped away. **So, point III is correct.** **So, all the three of the above factors contribute for making Earth a habitable planet.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the interior of Earth:
I. - The Sima zone is known for the surplus availability of acidic materials.
II. 	 The Sial zone is the source of magma and lava resources.
III. 	 The Nife zone is dominated by heavy metals.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Earth’s interior structure and composition are characterised by its thermal and physical state. It is divided into several distinct layers based on chemical composition and physical properties, including the crust (continental crust or Sial and oceanic crust or Sima), the mantle and the core (also called Nife).
● **The Sial:** It is made up of two words, i.e. Si plus and Al which denote **silica and aluminum.** It lies beneath the outer sedimentary envelope of the Earth’s internal structure. On average, its density is 2.9 and varies from 50 to 300 kilometers in its thickness. This layer is **characterised by the surplus** **availability of acid materials** along with silicates of potassium, sodium and aluminum, etc. Major structural parts of the planet Earth, i.e. continents are created on the sial layer. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● **The Sima:** A layer situated just below the sial is known as sima . It is made of **basalt material.** Sima is the **only provider of magma** and lava resources blown up from **volcanoes during active phases.** It is composed of two words Si plus and Ma, whereby Si stands for **silica and Ma for magnesium.** This layer is characterized by the availability of plenty of base materials including those of calcium, iron and magnesium silicates etc. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **The Nife:** It is placed beneath the middle layer of sima. Nickel (Ni) and Iron (Fe) are two dominant minerals. **Nife has a very high density because of the dominance of heavy metals.** Its thickness is 6880 kilometers. Availability of iron, i.e. ferrnium shows two properties of firmness and magnetism. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Places in News and Geopolitical Hotspots (Ukraine, Taiwan Strait, Hormuz, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** The Jovian planets do not have solid surfaces like the terrestrial planets.
**Statement II:** Jovian planets are primarily composed of methane and water vapour.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is not correct
options.d: Statement I is not correct but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The planets of the solar system are broadly classified into **terrestrial and Jovian planets** based on their position, composition and physical characteristics. **Mercury, Venus, Earth** and **Mars** are terrestrial planets, characterized by their rocky surfaces and proximity to the Sun. In contrast, **Jupiter,** **Saturn, Uranus** and **Neptune** are Jovian planets, known for their large sizes, gaseous compositions and location in the outer solar system.
● Unlike the **terrestrial planets** that make up our inner solar system, the **Jovian planets do not have** **solid surfaces.** Instead, they are **composed primarily of hydrogen and helium,** with traces of **methane, ammonia, water and other gases in their atmospheres**. These gases make up a deep atmosphere and become tightly compressed around relatively tiny cores of rock. At great depths within Jupiter, for example, the hydrogen gas is compacted so tightly that it exists in a rare metallic form. **So,** **statement I is correct and statement II is not correct.** **So, statement I is correct, but Statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● The **terrestrial planets,** meaning earth-like as they are made up of rock and metals and **have relatively high densities while** the **Jovian planets have low densities** because of their gaseous composition.
● **Terrestrial planets** possess **relatively thin atmospheres** composed mainly of gases like nitrogen, carbon dioxide and oxygen. Jovian planets are **enveloped by thick,** **massive atmospheres** dominated by hydrogen and helium, which contribute to their distinct gaseous appearance and larger size.
● **Terrestrial planets** like Mercury, Venus, Earth, and Mars **are characterized by** **their solid, rocky surfaces** composed primarily of silicate rocks and metals. These planets have well-defined crusts and mantles. In contrast, **Jovian planets** like Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune **lack a solid surface and are composed mainly of** **gases** (hydrogen and helium).
● **Terrestrial planets are small and dense due to their rocky composition.** **Jovian planets are much larger but have lower average densities** because they consist mainly of light gases like hydrogen and helium.
● **Jovian planets have significantly more moons compared to terrestrial** **planets**. For example, Jupiter has over 80 known moons, Saturn more than 80, Uranus 27, and Neptune 14, whereas terrestrial planets have very few: Earth has 1, Mars has 2 small moons, and Mercury and Venus have none**.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - In sedimentary rocks, the layers of deposits retain their characteristics even after lithification.
II. 	 In igneous rocks, the process of cooling and solidification can happen in the Earth’s crust or on the surface of the Earth.
III. 	 In metamorphism, rocks undergo recrystallisation and reorganisation of materials within the original rocks.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The rock cycle is a natural process where igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic rocks **constantly** **change** from one type to another over time due to **geological forces** like weathering, erosion, heat, pressure and tectonic activity.
● **Sedimentary Rocks:** Rocks (igneous, sedimentary and metamorphic) of the earth’s surface are exposed to denudational agents and are broken up into various sizes of fragments. Such fragments are transported by different exogenous agencies and deposited. These deposits through compaction turn into rocks. This process is called **lithification.** In many sedimentary rocks, the **layers of deposits retain their characteristics even after lithification. So, point I is** **correct.**
● **Igneous Rocks:** As igneous rocks form out of magma and lava from the interior of the Earth, they are known as primary rocks. The igneous rocks are formed when magma **cools and solidifies.** When magma in its upward movement cools and turns into solid form it is called igneous rock. **The process** **of cooling and solidification can happen in the Earth’s crust or on the surface of the Earth.** **So, point II is correct.**
● **Metamorphic Rocks:** The word metamorphic means ‘change of form’. These rocks form under the action of pressure, volume and temperature (PVT) changes. Metamorphism is a process by which already consolidated rocks **undergo recrystallisation and reorganisation of materials within the** **original rocks.** **So, point III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Various Types of Majority, Doctrines
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - Clastic sedimentary rocks are formed by the accumulation of materials derived from preexisting mechanically weathered rocks.
II. 	 Non-clastic sedimentary rocks are found in rocks that have precipitated chemically from water.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Sedimentary Rocks** are exposed to denudational agents and are broken up into various sizes of fragments. Such fragments are transported by different exogenous agencies and deposited. These deposits through compaction turn into rocks.
● Sedimentary Rocks are classified into two types - Clastic and  Non-clastic.
● Sedimentary rocks showing **clastic texture** consist of **discrete fragments and particles** that may be **cemented and compacted together.** They often result due to the **accumulation of materials** that originate and are transported as solid particles derived from **pre-existing rocks both** **mechanically and/or chemically weathered.** **So, statement I is correct.**
● The sedimentary rocks that they form are called **detrital or terrigenous** sedimentary rocks. For example, **gravel, sand and clay, etc.** Chemical sedimentary rocks, for example, coquina (limestone composed of shells and shell fragments), exhibit clastic texture. The same applies to some varieties of oolitic limestones.
● Some chemical sedimentary rocks have a **non-clastic or crystalline texture** in which the minerals form a pattern of interlocking crystals. These crystals may be microscopically small or large enough to be visible without magnification. **Non-clastic textures** are found mostly in rocks that **have** **precipitated chemically from water,** called chemical sedimentary rocks, such as **limestone,** **dolomite and chert. So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Orogenic movements
II. 	 Epeirogenic movements
III. 	 Earthquakes
IV. 	 Plate tectonic movements
How many of the above are considered part of diastrophism processes that build up portions of the Earth’s crust?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Diastrophism:** All processes that **move, elevate or build up portions of the Earth’s crust** come under diastrophism. They include:
○ **Orogenic processes** involving mountain building through severe folding affect long and narrow belts of the Earth’s crust. **So, point I is correct.** ○ **Epeirogenic processes** involving the uplift or warping of large parts of the Earth’s crust. **So,** **point II is correct.** ○ **Earthquakes** involving local relatively minor movements. **So, point III is correct.** ○ **Plate tectonics** involving horizontal movements of crustal plates. **So, point IV is correct.**
● In the process of orogeny, the **crust is severely deformed into folds.** Due to epeirogeny, there may be simple deformation. **Orogeny is a mountain-building process** whereas **epeirogeny is a** **continental building process.** Through the processes of orogeny, epeirogeny, earthquakes and plate tectonics, there can be faulting and fracturing of the crust. All these processes cause Pressure, Volume and Temperature (PVT) changes which in turn induce metamorphism of rocks. **So, all the four of the above are considered part of diastrophism processes that build up portions** **of the Earth’s crust.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the stages of a river and associated landforms, consider the following statements:
I. - V-shaped valleys are typical features formed during the youth stage of a river.
II. 	 Waterfalls are characteristic features of the mature stage of a river.
III. 	 Oxbow lakes are commonly formed during the old stage of a river.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **In humid regions,** which receive heavy rainfall, **running water** is considered the most important of the geomorphic agents in bringing about the degradation of the land surface. There are two components of running water. One is **overland flow** on the general land surface as a sheet. Another is **linear flow** as streams and rivers in valleys. Most of the erosional landforms made by running water are associated with vigorous and **youthful rivers flowing over steep gradients.**
● Rivers undergo a long journey from their source to mouth and during this journey, they pass through different stages of development — namely the **youth**, **mature** and **old stages**.
● **Youth stage:** Streams are few during this stage with poor integration and flow over original slopes showing shallow **V-shaped valleys with no floodplains** or with very narrow floodplains along trunk streams. Streams divide are broad and flat with marshes, swamps and lakes. Meanders, if present, develop over these broad upland surfaces. These meanders may eventually entrench themselves into the uplands. **Waterfalls** and **rapids** may exist where local hard rock bodies are exposed. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● **Mature stage:** During this stage, streams are plentiful with good integration. The valleys are still Vshaped but deep; trunk streams are broad enough to have wider floodplains within which **streams may** **flow in meanders** confined within the valley. The flat and broad interstream areas and swamps and marshes of youth disappear and the stream divides turn sharp. It must be noted here that, **waterfalls** **and rapids disappear** at this stage**. So, statement II is not correct.**
● **Old stage:** Smaller tributaries during old age are few with gentle gradients. Streams meander freely over vast floodplains showing **natural levees, oxbow lakes, etc.** Divides are broad and flat with lakes, swamps and marshes. Most of the landscape is at or slightly above sea level. **So, statement III is** **correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer**.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with respect to seismic waves:
I. - Primary (P) waves can travel through solid, liquid and gaseous media, with the highest velocity in solids.
II.	 Secondary (S) waves travel only through solids and disappear when passing through liquid regions.
III.	 The velocity of Surface (L) waves is higher than that of S waves but lower than that of P waves.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **All natural earthquakes take place in the** **lithosphere.** Earthquake waves are basically of two types — **body waves and surface waves.** Body waves are generated due to the **release of energy** **at the focus** and move in all directions travelling through the body of the earth. The body waves interact with the surface rocks and generate new sets of waves called **surface waves.** These waves move along the surface. The **velocity of waves** **changes as they travel through materials** with different densities.
**Characteristics of Seismic Waves:**
**Primary (P) Waves** **Secondary (S) Waves** **Surface/ Long (L) waves**
● Fastest wave and reaches first at the surface.
● Its velocity is lesser than Primary (P) waves. So it is recorded after P waves on the seismograph.
● Its velocity is lesser than P and S waves. So, it is recorded after P and S waves on the seismograph. **So,** **statement III is not** **correct.**
● Travels in solid, liquid and gas medium of matters.
● Travels only through the **solid state of** **matter.** **So,** **statement**
**II** **is** **correct.**
● Travels only through the solid state of matter
● **High velocity is in** **solid,** becomes less in liquid and very slow in gas state of matter. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● An increase in the density of rocks generally leads to an increase in the velocity of seismic waves, while a decrease in density results in a reduction in velocity.
● Velocity varies from
2.0 to 4.4 km per second.
● Change in the state of matter from solid to viscous or liquid, its velocity decreases even if the density is more.
● Once the state of matter changes from solid to viscous, its velocity is reduced. But when the rocks are melted, it disappears completely
● Velocity of P waves varies from 5.5 km per second at or near the surface to 13.0 km per second in the deep interior.
● Velocity of S waves varies from 3.25 km per second at or near the surface to 7.0 km per second in the interior.
**Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Origin, Evolution, Geological Time Scale
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to tectonic plate boundaries:
I. - Divergent plate boundaries are primarily associated with the formation of mid-oceanic ridges.
II. 	 At a convergent plate boundary, the subduction of the oceanic lithosphere can result in the formation of fold mountains.
III. 	 Transform boundaries neither create new lithosphere nor lead to subduction, but involve lateral movement of plates.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The theory of **Plate Tectonics** explains the large-scale motion of Earth's lithosphere, which is broken into several tectonic plates. These plates interact at their boundaries, leading to various geological features and phenomena. Based on their movement relative to one another, these boundaries are classified into **divergent**, **convergent** and **transform boundaries**. Each type of boundary is associated with specific tectonic activities such as **earthquakes, volcanic activity, mountain** **formation and crustal deformation**.
● At **divergent plate boundaries, rising convection currents in the Earth’s mantle bring hot,** **viscous rock material toward the surface**. This molten material forces its way up through a split created as tectonic plates move apart. As magma cools and solidifies, it forms a new crust, continuously pushing the large plates away from each other. **This process mainly occurs along mid-oceanic** **ridges**, making divergent boundaries also known as constructive boundaries because they create a new lithosphere. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Convergent plate boundaries** form where **convection currents push two tectonic plates** **toward each other.** When these plates collide, one plate may be forced beneath the other in a process called subduction, leading to the **destruction or reduction of the Earth’s surface area in that** **zone**. This boundary is therefore **termed destructive**. The intense pressure and folding of crustal material can result in the **formation of fold mountains** and volcanic activity. **So, statement II is** **correct.**
● **Transform boundaries** are characterized by plates **sliding laterally past one another.** Unlike divergent or convergent boundaries, transform boundaries **neither create new lithosphere nor** **cause subduction or destruction of crust**. These boundaries are often narrow zones or lines separating plates, known as plate boundaries, while the surrounding areas are called plate margins. Plate boundaries can be oceanic or continental, depending on their location relative to the Earth's surface. **So,** **statement III is correct.**
**Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
Consider the following information:
Type of Plateau Characteristic Example
I. - Intermontane Bordered by the fold mountain ranges
Deccan Plateau
II. - Piedmont Situated at the foot of the mountains
Malwa Plateau
III. - Continental Formed by the spread of horizontal lava sheets
Volcanic lava plateau of Maharashtra
In which of the above rows is the given information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Plateau or Plateaus** is an extensive area of **flat upland** usually bounded by **steep slopes** on all sides but sometimes **enclosed by mountains.** The essential criteria for plateaus are low relative relief and some altitude. It covers about **18% of the earth’s surface.** This landform has a large elevated area on its top unlike a mountain and has nearly an even surface out there.
● On the basis of their geographical location and structure of rocks, the plateaus can be classified as: ○ **Intermontane Plateau:** The plateau which are **bordering the fold mountain** range or are **partly or fully enclosed** within them are the intermontane plateaus. Vertical movements raise this extensive landform of nearly horizontal rocks to thousands of metres above sea level. The extensive and over 4500 metres **high plateau of Tibet** is one such example. It is surrounded by folded mountains like **Himalaya, Karakoram, Kunlun, Tien Shan on its two sides.** The **plateau of Colorado** is another well-known example, over one km high into which rivers have cut the Grand Canyon and a series of gorges. The plateau of Mexico, Bolivia and Iran are all other examples of this type & platines. **So, row I is not correctly matched.**
■ The **Deccan Plateau** is a classic example of a **volcanic or lava plateau,** formed
millions of years ago by repeated outpourings of basaltic lava that solidified into a vast, elevated landform. ○ **Piedmont Plateau:** The plateaus that are **situated at the foot of the mountains** and are bounded on other sides by a plain or an ocean are called piedmont plateau, The **plateau of** **Malwa in India,** Patagonia facing the Atlantic Ocean and the Appalachians situated between the Appalachian Mountain and the Atlantic Coastal Plain in U.S.A are their examples. In their case, the areas once high have now been reduced by various agents of erosion. For this reason, these are also called the plateaus of denudation. **So, row II is correctly matched.** ○ **Continental Plateau:** These are formed either by an **extensive continental uplift** or **by the** **spread of horizontal basic lava sheets** completely covering the original topography to a great depth. The **volcanic lava covered plateau of Maharashtra** in India; Snake River Plateau in North West USA are the examples of this type. These are also called the plateau of accumulation. **All continental plateaus show an abrupt elevation** in contrast to the nearby lowland or the sea. As compared to others, these plateaus cover a vast area like the Great Indian Plateau and those of Arabia, Spain, Greenland, Africa and Australia. **So, row III is correctly matched.** **So, in only two of the above rows is the given information correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer**.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Distribution of Continents and Oceans
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - The matching of continents
II. 	 Rocks of same age across the oceans
III. 	 The occurrence of rich placer deposits of gold in the Ghana coast
IV. 	 Identical species of plants and animals found on either side of the marine barriers
How many of the above support the Continental Drift theory?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Continental drift** describes one of the earliest ways geologists thought continents moved over time. This map displays an early **"supercontinent,"** Gondwana, which eventually moved to form the continents we know today. Fossils of similar organisms across widely disparate continents encouraged the revolutionary theory of continental drift.
● Various evidence supports this theory of continental drift: ○ **The Matching of Continents (Jig-Saw-Fit):** The **shorelines of Africa and South** **America** facing each other **have a remarkable and unmistakable match.** It may be noted that a map produced using a computer programme to find the best fit of the Atlantic margin was presented by Bullard in 1964. It proved to be quite perfect. The match was tried at 1,000- fathom line instead of the present shoreline. **So, point I is correct.** ○ **Rocks of Same Age Across the Oceans**: The radiometric dating methods developed in the recent period have facilitated correlating the rock formation from different continents across the vast ocean. The belt of ancient rocks of 2,000 million years from Brazil coast matches with those from western Africa. The earliest **marine deposits along the coastline of South America** **and Africa are of the Jurassic age.** This suggests that the ocean did not exist prior to that time. **So, point II is correct.** ○ **Placer Deposits:** The occurrence of **rich placer deposits of gold in the Ghana coast** and the **absolute absence of source rock in the region** is an amazing fact. The gold bearing veins are in Brazil and it is obvious that the gold deposits of the Ghana are derived from the Brazil plateau when the two continents lay side by side. **So, statement III is correct.** ○ **Distribution of Fossils:** When **identical species of plants and animals** adapted to living on land or in fresh water are f**ound on either side of the marine barriers,** a problem arises regarding accounting for such distribution. The observations that Lemurs occur in **India,** **Madagascar and Africa** led some to consider a contiguous landmass ‘Lemuria’ linking these three landmasses. Mesosaurus was a small reptile adapted to shallow brackish water. The skeletons of these are found only in two localities: the **Southern Cape province of South** **Africa and Iraver formations of Brazil.** The two localities are presently 4,800 km apart with an ocean in between them.  **So, point IV is correct.**
**So, all the four of the above support the Continental Drift theory.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Tillite:** It is the sedimentary rock formed out of deposits of glaciers. The **Gondwana** **system of sediments from India** is known to have its counterparts in **six different** **landmasses of the Southern Hemisphere.** At the base, the system has thick tillite indicating extensive and prolonged glaciation. Counterparts of this succession are found in Africa, Falkland Island, Madagascar, Antarctica and Australia. Overall resemblance of the Gondwana-type sediments clearly demonstrates that these landmasses had remarkably similar histories. The glacial tillite provides unambiguous evidence of palaeoclimates and also of drifting of continents.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText: Which one of the following best describes the term ‘Abyssal Plains’?

options.a: Gently sloping plains formed at the base of mountains due to weathering and erosion
options.b: Extensive oceanic flat plains located between continental margins and mid-oceanic ridges
options.c: Vast plains formed by volcanic lava flows in basaltic regions
options.d: Rolling plains formed due to prolonged erosion in humid climates

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The ocean water conceals a considerable variety of landscapes very similar to those on the continents. There are mountains, basins, plateaus, ridges, canyons and trenches beneath the ocean water too. Ocean basins are broadly divided into four major sub-divisions. They are:
○ Continental shelf ○ Continental slope ○ **Abyssal plains** ○ The ocean depths.
● **Abyssal Plain:** These are extremely **flat and featureless plains** of the deep-ocean floor. These are **extensive plains that lie between the continental margins and mid-oceanic ridges.** In fact, the abyssal plains are likely the most level areas on the Earth. Abyssal plains cover a major portion of the ocean floor between depths of 3000m to 6000m. They have extensive submarine plateaus, hills, guyots and seamounts.
● The floor of the **abyssal plain is covered by sediments.** The plains close to the continents are covered mostly by sediments brought down from the land. But those seas which favour an abundant growth of organisms have a thick layer of sediments, formed from the remains of living things. **These** **sediments are called oozes.** Some of the open seas do not support enough life to produce ooze on the floor. They are covered with a type of sediment called red clay, which is of volcanic origin or made up of tiny particles brought by wind and rivers.
**Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with respect to alluvial fans and bajadas:
I. - An alluvial fan results from sediment flowing from steep mountain creeks and depositing in flat, wide valleys.
II. 	 A bajada is the convergence of many alluvial fans.
III. 	 Both Alluvial fans and bajadas are found only in deserts.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Alluvial fans** and **bajadas** are depositional landforms commonly found in **arid and semi-arid regions**, but they can also occur in other geomorphological contexts. These features result from the action of **water flowing** **down from mountains or highlands**, carrying sediments that are deposited when the flow loses energy.
● **Alluvial fans:** When rivers flow down the **steep** **mountain slopes** into the **valleys they drop their** **load of coarse** - sand and gravels as their sudden decrease in velocity. The load deposited generally acquires a fan-like shape; therefore they are called alluvial fan. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **A bajada is the convergence, or blending, of many alluvial fans.** Bajadas are common in dry climates, such as the canyons of the American Southwest. Bajadas can be narrow, from the flow of two or three streams of water, or they can be wide, where dozens of alluvial fans converge. **So, statement** **II is correct.**
● **Alluvial fans and bajadas are often found in deserts,** where flash floods wash alluvium down from nearby hills. They can also be found in **wetter climates,** where streams are more common. Alluvial fans are **even found underwater.** A subaqueous fan is created as an underwater current deposits alluvium from a submarine hill or glacier. **So, statement III is not correct.**
● Sometimes, **fans are formed without the aid of water.** These are called **colluvial fans.** Colluvial fans are created by mass wasting. Mass wasting is simply the downward movement of rock, soil, or other material. Alluvium is material transported by water, while colluvium is material transported by mass wasting. Landslides are an instance of mass wasting that often create colluvial fans. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Solifluction is most commonly observed in which
of the following conditions?

options.a: Arid deserts with high diurnal range
options.b:
Moist temperate regions with frequent
surface thawing and rainfall
options.c:
Tropical rainforests with dense vegetation
cover
options.d: Coastal areas affected by tidal erosion

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Slow Movements:**
● **Solifluction** is a type of slow mass movement that occurs when **water-saturated soil or fine-** **grained debris** slowly flows downslope. This process is most common in **moist temperate regions,** especially where **surface thawing of frozen ground** (such as during seasonal melting) and **prolonged** **rainfall are frequent.** These conditions lead to oversaturation of the upper soil layer, while the lower layers remain impervious, triggering the flow.
**Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Match the following erosion processes with their
correct descriptions:
List I (Erosion Process)
List II (Description)
I. - Attrition A. Removal and transport of loose sand and dust by wind in arid regions
II. - Hydraulic Action B. Mechanical wear of rock fragments due to mutual collision during transport
III. Deflation C. Rock disintegration by water pressure without involvement of solid erosion tools
IV. Corrasion D. Dissolution of the soluble materials through the process of disintegration

options.a: I - A, II - B, III - C, IV - D
options.b: I - B, II - C, III - A, IV - D
options.c: I - C, II - B, III - D, IV - A
options.d: I - A, II - C, III - D, IV - B

questions.explanationMarkdown:
Exogenic processes are generally called erosional processes which perform three-phase work i.e. erosion, transportation and deposition. Erosion is the acquisition and transportation of rock debris by geomorphic agents like running water, the wind, waves, glaciers, etc.  The erosional work by different processes is performed through the mechanism of: Corrasion or Abrasion,  Corrosion or solution, Attrition, Hydraulic action and Deflation.
● **Attrition** refers to **mechanical tear and wear of erosion** tools suffered by themselves. The boulders, cobbles, pebbles etc. while moving downstream with **water collide against each other and** **thus are fragmented into smaller and finer pieces in the transit.** Attrition by wind involves mechanical breakdown of rock particles while they are transported by wind through the processes of saltation and surface creep.
● The breakdown of rocks due to **pressure exerted by water currents of the rivers and sea waves** is termed as **Hydraulic action.** It is the mechanical loosening and removal of materials of rocks by water alone **without the help of erosion tools.** The rivers erode their valley walls through hydraulic action.
● **Deflation** is the process of removing, lifting and blowing away **dry and loose particles of sands and** **dust by winds.** It happens in **semi-arid or arid regions.** Congelifraction (frost weathering), Congelifluction (soil creep), nivation (snow patch erosion), etc. are significant weathering and transportation mechanisms performed by periglacial agents.
● **Corrasion or Abrasion** involves the removal of loosened materials of the rocks by different erosional processes in different manner. Erosion tools refer to all those solid materials (boulders, cobbles, pebbles, sands etc.) with the help of which erosional agents attack and abrade the rocks. The mechanism of corrosion involves **dissolution of the soluble materials through the process of disintegration** and decomposition of carbonate rocks, which is effectively corroded by running water, groundwater and sea waves.
**Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to igneous rocks:
I. - Intrusive igneous rocks are formed from magma that cools and solidifies beneath the Earth's surface.
II. 	 Plutonic rocks typically have a fine-grained texture due to rapid cooling.
III. 	 Dolerite is an example of an intrusive igneous rock that forms at shallow depths.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Igneous rocks** are formed from **solidified molten magma below or on the Earth’s surface.** As they comprise the earth’s first crust and all other rocks are derived from them, these are called the parent of all rocks or the **‘primary rocks’.**
● On the basis of their mode of occurrence, igneous rocks can be classified as extrusive or volcanic rocks and intrusive rocks.
○ **Intrusive igneous rocks are created when molten magma cools and solidifies below** **the Earth’s surface**. The cooling process happens slowly due to the insulating effect of the overlying rocks, which leads to the formation of large, visible crystals within the rock. This slow cooling is a defining characteristic of intrusive rocks. **So, statement I is correct.** ○ **Plutonic rocks** are a type of intrusive igneous rock that form deep within the Earth’s crust. **Because of the depth and** the resulting **slow cooling process, these rocks develop a** **coarse-grained texture**, with large and easily visible crystals. **So, statement II is not** **correct.** ○ Deep-seated intrusive rocks are termed as plutonic rocks and shallow-depth intrusive rocks are termed hypabyssal. **Granite and dolerite are common examples of intrusive rocks.** From this point of view, therefore, igneous rocks can, in accordance with their mode of formation, be classified as (a) Plutonic, (b) Hypabyssal and (c) Volcanic rock masses. The huge blocks of coarse granitic rocks are found both in the Himalaya and the Deccan Plateau. **So, statement III is** **correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the volcanic landforms:
I. - Composite volcanoes are characterised by eruptions of more viscous lavas than basalt.
II. 	 Calderas can only result from less explosive volcanic activity.
III. 	 The Deccan Traps of India are an example of a flood basalt province.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Volcanoes are classified on the basis of the nature of eruption and the form developed at the surface.
● **Composite Volcanoes**: These volcanoes are **characterised by** **eruptions of cooler and more** **viscous lavas than basalt.** These volcanoes often result in explosive eruptions. Along with lava, large quantities of pyroclastic material and ashes find their way to the ground. This material accumulates in the vicinity of the vent openings leading to the formation of layers and this makes the mounts appear as composite volcanoes. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● **Caldera:** These are the **most explosive of** **the Earth’s volcanoes**. They are a large depression formed when a **volcano erupts** **and collapses.** During a volcanic eruption, magma present in the magma chamber underneath the volcano is expelled, often forcefully. When the magma chamber empties, the support that the magma had provided inside the chamber disappears.
○ **Calderas such as Crater Lake** and those in Yellowstone National Park **result from dramatic eruptions**, but **slower eruptions can also** **create calderas.** This often occurs with **shield volcanoes**, which are typically flatter and more gradually sloped. Lava flows from shield volcanoes more slowly and often at regular intervals. **So,** **statement II is not correct.**
● **Flood Basalt Provinces:** These volcanoes outpour highly fluid lava that flows for long distances. Some parts of the world are covered by thousands of sq. km of thick basalt lava flows. There can be a **series** **of flows with some flows attaining a thickness of more than 50 m.** Individual flows may extend for hundreds of km. The **Deccan Traps from India,** presently covering most of the Maharashtra plateau, are a much larger flood basalt province. It is believed that initially, the trap formations covered a much larger area than the present. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText: They are a large body of magmatic material that cools in the deeper depths of the crust and develops in the form of large domes. They appear on the surface only after the denudational processes remove the overlying materials. The Karnataka plateau with domal hills of granite rocks is an example of it. This is the most likely description of:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Batholiths** are huge masses of solidified magma. They are a **large body of** **magmatic material** that **cools in the** **deeper depths** of the crust and develops in the form of **large domes.** They appear on the surface only after the **denudational processes** remove the overlying materials.
● They vary in size; some are as much as several hundred kilometers across and thousands of kilometers thick. They generally form the core of the major mountains. Their irregular dome-shaped roofs sometimes appear on the surface after erosion over millions of years. The **Karnataka plateau** is spotted with domal hills of granite rocks. Most of these, now exfoliated, are examples of **laccoliths or batholiths.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Laccoliths** are large, dome-shaped intrusive bodies with a level base and connected by a **pipe-like conduit from below.** It resembles the surface volcanic domes of composite volcanoes, only these are located at deeper depths. It can be regarded as the localised source of lava that finds its way to the surface.
● When the lava makes its way through cracks and the fissures developed in the land, it solidifies almost **perpendicular to the ground.** It gets cooled in the same position to develop a **wall-like structure.** Such structures are called **dykes**. These are the most commonly found intrusive forms in the **western** **Maharashtra area.** These are considered the feeders for the eruptions that led to the development of the Deccan traps.
● The near **horizontal bodies** of the intrusive igneous rocks are called a **sill or** **sheet,** depending on the thickness of the material. The thinner ones are called sheets, while the thick horizontal deposits are called **sills.**
● **Lapolith and Phacolith:** As and when the lava moves upwards, a portion of the same may tend to move in a horizontal direction wherever it finds a weak plane. It may be expressed in different forms. In case it develops into a **saucer shape, concave to the sky body,** it is called **lapolith**. A wavy mass of intrusive rocks, at times, is found at the base of synclines or at the top of anticlines in folded igneous country. Such wavy materials have a **definite conduit to the source beneath in the form of magma chambers** (subsequently developed as batholiths). These are called the **phacoliths.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Movement of Ocean Water (Tides, Waves, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Juan de Fuca
II. 	 Nazca Plate
III. 	 Philippine Plate
IV. 	 Somali Plate
How many of the above tectonic plates are located along the Ring of Fire?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Ring of Fire** is a roughly 25,000-mile chain of volcanoes and seismically active sites that outline the **Pacific Ocean.** The Ring of Fire traces the meeting points of many tectonic plates, including the Eurasian, North American, **Juan de Fuca**, Cocos, Caribbean, **Nazca**, Antarctic, Indian, Australian, **Philippine** and other smaller plates, which all encircle the large Pacific Plate. **So, points I, II and III** **are correct.**
● The plates are constantly sliding past, colliding into, or moving above or below each other. This movement results in **deep ocean trenches, volcanic eruptions and earthquake epicentres** along the boundaries where the plates meet, called fault lines.
○ **The Somali Plate** is part of the African tectonic system and is located in the eastern part of Africa. It is currently moving away from the Nubian Plate, forming the East African Rift System. This region is tectonically active but is **not located along the Pacific Ring of Fire. So, point** **IV is not correct.**
● The tectonic activity along the Ring of Fire also results in about **90% of the world’s earthquakes,** including the Valdivia Earthquake of Chile in 1960, the strongest recorded earthquake at 9.5 out of 10 on the Richter scale.
● The Ring of Fire is also where an estimated 75% of the planet’s volcanoes are located, such as Mount Tambora of Indonesia, which erupted in 1815 and became the largest volcanic eruption in recorded history.
**So, only three of the above tectonic plates are located along the Ring of Fire.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements correctly describes a plate boundary associated with the Indian Plate?

options.a: The northern boundary is a divergent plate boundary characterized by the forming of the Himalayas.
options.b: The western boundary extends along the Rakinyoma Mountains of Myanmar towards the island arc along the Java Trench.
options.c: The southern boundary with the Antarctic plate is a convergent margin merging near New Zealand.
options.d: The eastern margin to the east of Australia is a spreading site in the form of an oceanic ridge.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Indian plate includes **Peninsular India and the** **Australian continental portions.** The subduction zone along the **Himalayas forms the northern plate** **boundary in the form of continent—continent** **convergence.**
● In the east, it extends through the Rakinyoma Mountains of Myanmar towards the island arc along the Java Trench. The **eastern margin is a spreading site lying to the** **east of Australia** in the form of **an oceanic ridge** in the SW Pacific.
● Its **western margin follows Kirthar Mountain of** **Pakistan.** It further extends along the Makrana coast and joins the **spreading site** from the Red Sea rift southeastward along the Chagos Archipelago. The boundary between **India and the Antarctic plate** is also marked by an **oceanic ridge (divergent boundary)** running in roughly W-E direction and merging into the spreading site, a little south of **New Zealand.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** The concentration of water vapour in the atmosphere allows the Earth neither to become too cold nor too hot.
**Statement II:** Water vapour absorbs parts of the insolation from the sun and preserves the Earth’s radiated heat.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is not correct
options.d: Statement I is not correct but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Water vapour** present in the air is known as humidity. The actual amount of water vapour present in the atmosphere is known as the absolute humidity. The ability of the air to hold water vapour depends entirely on its temperature.
● **Water vapour** is also a **variable gas** in the atmosphere, which **decreases with altitude.** In the **warm and wet tropics**, it may account for **four per cent of the air by volume,** while in the **dry** **and cold areas of desert and polar regions,** it may be less than **one per cent of the air.** Water vapour also decreases from the equator towards the poles. It also absorbs parts of the **insolation from** **the sun and preserves the Earth’s radiated heat**. It thus acts like a blanket, **allowing the Earth** **neither to become too cold nor too hot.** Water vapour also contributes to the stability and instability in the air. **So, statements I and II are correct.** **So, both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Which of the following factors are responsible for variation in the horizontal distribution of pressure?
I. - Air temperature
II. 	 The Earth’s rotation
III. 	 Presence of water vapour
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The atmosphere is held on the Earth by the **gravitational pull of the Earth.** A column of air exerts weight in terms of pressure on the surface of the Earth. The weight of the column of air at a given place and time is called **air pressure or atmospheric pressure.**
● The distribution of atmospheric pressure on the surface of the Earth is not uniform. It varies both vertically and horizontally.
○ **Horizontal Distribution:** The distribution of atmospheric pressure over the globe is known as the horizontal distribution of pressure. It is shown with the help of isobars. An isobar is a line connecting points that have equal values of pressure. ○ The horizontal distribution of **atmospheric pressure is not uniform in the world**. It varies from time to time at a given place; it varies from place to place over short distances. The factors responsible for variation in the horizontal distribution of pressure are as follows:
■ **Air temperature:** Air temperature and pressure share an inverse relationship. An
increase in temperature leads to a decrease in air pressure. When air is heated, it expands, becomes less dense and rises, resulting in lower pressure. This explains why equatorial regions experience low pressure, forming a zone known as the "equatorial low" or "doldrums," while polar areas maintain relatively higher pressure. **So, point I is correct.** ■ **The Earth’s rotation:** The Earth's rotation creates centrifugal force, causing air to
deflect and pressure to decrease. This leads to the formation of sub-polar low-pressure belts and sub-tropical high-pressure belts. It also causes air to converge and diverge. Convergence results in low pressure, while divergence leads to high pressure. **So, point** **II is correct.** ■ **Presence of water vapour:** Air with **higher quantity of water vapour** has **lower**
**pressure** and that with lower quantity of water vapour has higher pressure. In winter the continents are relatively cool and tend to develop high pressure centres; in summer they stay warmer than the oceans and tend to be dominated by low pressure, conversely, the oceans are associated with low pressure in winter and high pressure in summer. **So,** **point III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Insurance and Pension
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements regarding the Coriolis force:
I. - It is maximum at the equator and decreases towards the poles.
II. 	 It influences the velocity but not the speed of moving objects.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● All measurements are relative to some standard. Motion is measured relative to a **reference frame.** It is common to assume that this reference frame is at rest, or moving at constant velocity with respect to the fixed stars. Newton's laws of motion are defined with respect to a fixed reference frame.
● **Newton's laws of motion apply strictly to a fixed,** **inertial reference frame**. However, since the Earth itself is in motion, an apparent force must be introduced to account for the effects of this rotating, non-inertial reference frame. This apparent force is known as the **Coriolis force**.
● The **magnitude and direction** of the Coriolis force are determined by several factors. ○ It is directly proportional to the speed of the moving object, such as wind. **When the wind** **speed is zero, there is no relative motion** and consequently, the Coriolis force is absent. ○ As an object’s speed increases, its inertia also increases, requiring a greater force to alter its direction. Accordingly, the Coriolis force becomes stronger with increasing wind speed.
● The Coriolis force acts **perpendicular to the direction of motion**—to the **right in the Northern** **Hemisphere** and to the **left in the Southern Hemisphere**—and thus **cannot alter the wind's** **speed.** It does not generate wind; rather, it influences only the direction of existing motion. **So,** **statement II is correct.**
● Additionally, the magnitude of the Coriolis force **varies with latitude**. It is **zero at the equator** and **increases progressively toward the poles**. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **The Coriolis Effect and Wind:** Winds do not cross the isobars at right angles as the **pressure** **gradient directs them**. They get deflected from **their original paths.** One of the most potent influences on wind direction is the deflection caused by the Earth’s rotation on its axis. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Atmospheric phenomenon Description
I. - Conduction Heat transfer through direct contact between Earth's surface and atmosphere
II. - Convection Vertical transfer of heat through rising and sinking air
III. Radiation Transfer of heat energy through the empty space
IV. Advection Horizontal transport of heat by winds
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● There are four heating processes directly responsible for heating the atmosphere. They are **Radiation,** **Conduction, Convection and Advection.**
● **Conduction:** When two objects of unequal temperature come in contact with each other, **heat** **energy flows from the warmer object to the cooler object** and this process of heat transfer is known as conduction. The flow continues till the temperature of both the objects becomes equal or the contact is broken. The conduction in the atmosphere occurs at the **zone of contact between the** **atmosphere and the Earth’s surface. So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● **Convection:** Transfer of heat by **movement of a mass or substance from one place to another,** **generally vertical,** is called convection. The air of the lower layers of the atmosphere gets heated either by the Earth’s radiation or by conduction. The heating of the air leads to its expansion. Its density decreases and it moves upwards. **Continuous ascent of heated air creates a vacuum in the lower** **layers of the atmosphere.** As a consequence, cooler air comes down to fill the vacuum, leading to convection. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● **Radiation:** It is the process by which solar energy reaches the Earth and the Earth loses energy to outer space. When the source of heat transmits heat directly to an object through heat waves, it is known as a radiation process. **In this process, heat travels through the empty space.** The vast amount of heat energy coming to and leaving the Earth is in the form of radiation. **So, pair III is correctly** **matched.**
● **Advection:** Winds carry the temperature of one place to another. The temperature of a place will rise if it lies on the path of winds coming from warmer regions. The temperature will fall if the place lies on the path of the winds blowing from cold regions. This process of **horizontal transport of heat by** **winds is known as advection. So, pair IV is correctly matched.** **So, all the four of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:**
India’s northwestern plains experience rainfall
and hailstorms during the winter season.
**Statement II:**
In winters, jet streams drive western disturbances
from the Mediterranean region into India.
Which one of the following is correct in
respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is not
correct
options.d:
Statement I is not correct but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The changes in the upper air circulation over the Indian landmass are the cause for the sudden outbreak of monsoons in India. Jet streams in the upper air system influence the climate of India. **Jet streams** are currents of air high above Earth. They move **eastward at altitudes of about 8 to 15 kilometers**. They form where large temperature differences exist in the atmosphere. **The Westerly Jet Stream and its Impact:**
● During winter, it is about 8 km. above sea level, a westerly jet stream blows at a very high speed over the **subtropical zone.** The Himalayan ranges bifurcate this jet stream. This jet stream's northern branch blows along the north edge of this barrier. The southern branch blows eastwards, south of the Himalayan ranges, along the **25° N latitude.** Meteorologists believe that this branch of the jet stream significantly influences India's winter weather conditions. This **jet stream** is responsible for bringing **western** **disturbances from the Mediterranean region** into the Indian subcontinent. **Winter rain and hail** **storms in the northwestern plains** and occasional heavy snowfall in hilly regions are caused by these disturbances. Cold waves generally follow these in the whole of the northern plains. **So, statements I** **and II are correct.** **So, both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.** **Subject:** Geography | Climate Weather and Atmosphere **Tags:** Conceptual, Analytical

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Type of cloud Description
I. - Cirrostratus Resembles a thin white sheet or veil
II. - Stratocumulus Fibrous towering cloud with absence of dark parts
III. Nimbostratus Grey cloud layer producing continuous rain
IV. Cumulus Vertical cloud with a rounded top and horizontal base
How many of the above pairs are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Cloud Formation:**
● When air rises, it cools due to expansion. Once the dew point is reached, further cooling causes **condensation of water vapour in the atmosphere.** This results in the formation of **tiny water** **droplets,** each less than 0.001 cm in radius. These droplets are too small to fall as rain or snow and remain suspended in the air, appearing as clouds. The classification of clouds is based on a combination of form, height and appearance:
● **Cirrostratus (Cs):** This resembles a thin **white sheet or veil;** the sky looks milky and the sun or moon shines through it with a **characteristic 'halo'.** **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● **Stratocumulus (St-Cu):** **Grey or whitish,** or both grey and whitish, patch, sheet or layer of cloud that **almost always has dark parts,** composed of tessellations, rounded masses, rolls, etc., which are **non-fibrous (except for virga)** and which may or may not be merged; most of the regularly arranged small elements have an apparent width of more than 5°. **So, pair II is not correctly matched.**
● **Nimbostratus (Ni-St): Grey cloud layer, often dark**, the appearance of which is rendered diffuse by **more or less continuously falling rain or snow,** which, in most cases, reaches the ground. It is thick enough throughout to blot out the Sun. Low, ragged clouds frequently occur below the layer, with
which they may or may not merge. **So, pair III is correctly matched.**
● **Cumulus (Cu):** This is a **vertical cloud with a rounded top and horizontal base**, typical of humid tropical regions, associated with up-rising convectional currents. Its great white globular masses may look grey against the sun but it is a ‘fair weather cloud'. **So, pair IV is correctly matched.**
**So, only three of the above pairs are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
● **High-Level Clouds: Cirrus** (Ci), **cirrocumulus** (Cc) and **cirrostratus** (Cs) are high-level clouds. They are typically thin and white in appearance, but can appear in a magnificent array of colors when the sun is low on the horizon.
● **Mid-Level Clouds: Altocumulus** (Ac), **altostratus** (As) and **nimbostratus** (Ns) are mid-level clouds composed primarily of water droplets. However, they can be composed of ice crystals when temperatures are low enough.
● **Low-Level Clouds: Cumulus** (Cu), **stratocumulus** (Sc), **stratus** (St) and **cumulonimbus** (Cb) are low clouds composed of water droplets. **Cumulonimbus**, with its strong vertical updraft, extends well into the high-level clouds.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** In January, low pressure areas develop over South America and Southern Africa.
**Statement II:** In January, there is northward shift of equatorial low-pressure belt due to apparent movement of the Sun.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is not correct
options.d: Statement I is not correct but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The variation of pressure from **place to place** and from **season to season** over the earth plays an important role in affecting the weather and climate. In the pressure distribution through isobars, the pressures at all places are reduced to sea level to avoid the effect of altitude on air pressure.
● **In January,** with the **south-ward apparent movement of the Sun,** the equatorial low pressure belt shifts a little south of the mean equatorial position. Areas of lowest pressure occur in **South** **America, Southern Africa and Australia.** This is because the **land tends to get hotter rapidly** **than water.** Subtropical high pressure cells are centered over the ocean in the southern hemisphere. The belt of high pressure is interrupted by the continental land masses where the temperature is much higher. **So, statement I is correct and statement II is not correct.**
**So, statement I is correct, but Statement II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● In the **northern hemisphere**, ridges of high pressure occur in the **subtropical** **latitudes over the continent.** A well developed high pressure cell occurs in the interior parts of Eurasia. This is due to the fact that **land cools more rapidly than** **oceans.** Its temperatures are lower in winter than the surrounding seas. In the southern hemisphere, the subpolar low pressure belt circles the earth as a real belt of low pressure and is not divided into cells, because there is virtually no landmass. In the northern hemisphere two cells of low pressure namely Iceland low and Aleutian low develop over the North Atlantic and the North Pacific oceans respectively.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
Consider the following information:
Wind Type Region
I. - Chinook Warm U.S.A. and Canada
II. - Harmattan Cold Gulf of Mexico
III. Zonda Warm Argentina
IV. Mistral Cold France
In how many of the above rows is the given information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Wind is the movement of air, caused by the uneven heating of the Earth by the sun and the Earth's own rotation. The movement of winds between **high and low pressure systems** within smaller areas is called **local winds.** These winds are also divided on the basis of temperature, e.g. **warm and cold** **winds**.
● **Chinook:** It is the name of a **hot and dry local wind** which moves down the eastern slopes of the **Rockies in the U.S.A. and Canada.** The literal meaning of chinook is ‘snow eater’ as they help in melting the snow earlier. They keep the grasslands clear of snow. Hence, they are very helpful to ranchers. **So, row I is correctly matched.**
● **Harmattan:** It is a **hot, dry, dusty wind** blowing from the **Sahara desert over West Africa** into the **Gulf of Guinea** where it is called ‘Guinea Doctor’ because it reduces the high humidity of the western coast of Africa (Guinea). **So, row II is not correctly matched.**
● **Zonda:** It is also called Sondo, winter Foehn. It is a **warm, dry wind** blowing east of the Andes in **Argentina**. It becomes more vigorous during the winter season. **So, row III is correctly matched.**
● **Mistral:** They are the most common **local cold winds.** They originate in the **Alps and move over** **France towards the Mediterranean Sea** through the Rhone valley. They are very **cold, dry and** **high-velocity winds**. They bring down the temperature below the freezing point in areas of their influence. People in these areas protect their orchards and gardens by growing thick hedges and building their houses facing the Mediterranean sea. **So, row IV is correctly matched.** **So, in only three of the above rows is the given information correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Other important local winds:**
● **Foehn:** It is a strong**, dusty, dry and warm** local wind which develops on the leeward side of the **Alps mountain ranges.** Regional pressure gradient forces the air to ascend and cross the barrier. Ascending air sometimes causes precipitation on the windward side of the mountains. After crossing the mountain crest, the Foehn winds starts descending on the leeward side or northern slopes of the mountain as warm and dry wind.
● **Sirocco:** It is a **warm, dry and dusty** local wind, which blows in northerly direction from the **Sahara desert** and after crossing the **Mediterranean Sea**, reaches even upto Italy and Spain. It becomes extremely warm and dry while descending through the northern slopes of **Atlas Mountain.** It is known as **Khamsin in Egypt**, **Gibli in** **Libya, Chili in Tunisia,** **Simoom in Arabian Desert**, **Blood Rain in South Italy,** **Leveche in Spain** and **Gharbi in the Adriatic and Aegean Sea.**
● **Blizzards:** These are **violent, stormy and extremely cold polar winds** laden with dry snow and are prevalent in north and south Polar Regions, Siberia, Canada and some parts of USA.
● **Loo:** These are **hot and dry winds that blow over the northern plains of India** during the months of May and June. They usually occur during the afternoons and their direction is generally from west to east.

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Places in News and Geopolitical Hotspots (Ukraine, Taiwan Strait, Hormuz, etc.)
questions.questionText: Which one of the following layers of the atmosphere has the lowest recorded temperature?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Earth's atmosphere is divided into several layers: the troposphere, stratosphere, mesosphere and thermosphere. Each layer has distinct temperature characteristics.
● **The mesosphere** is the third layer of the atmosphere
which **extends up to 80 kms.**  The temperature in the mesosphere **continuously decreases with increasing** **height.** The top of the mesosphere is the **coldest area** **of the Earth's atmosphere** because temperature may locally **decrease to as low as 100 K (-173°C).** Meteors occur in the mesosphere and "Noctilucent" clouds are formed by the process of condensation in association with meteoric dust. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Distribution of Continents and Oceans
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - Planetary winds blow in the same direction
from high pressure belts to low pressure belts
throughout the year.
II. 	 The trade winds are easterlies blowing from
sub-tropical high-pressure areas towards
equatorial low-pressure areas.
III. 	 The direction of polar easterlies in the
northern hemisphere is from southeast to
northwest.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Winds play an important role in understanding the climatic characteristics of different parts of the world and our surroundings. There is a closer relationship between winds and the belt of atmospheric pressure.
● **Planetary winds:** The planetary winds blow from **high pressure areas to low pressure areas** in the **same direction throughout the year.** These winds blow extensively over the continent and oceans. They are known as the easterlies or trade winds, westerlies and polar easterlies. **So, statement** **I is correct.**
● **The Easterlies or Trade winds:** The Easterlies winds blow from **sub-tropical high-pressure areas** **towards equatorial low-pressure areas.** It is also known as the **trade winds.** The direction of these winds in the northern hemisphere is from northeast to southwest and southern hemisphere southeast to northwest. The deflection of winds is a result of the **Coriolis Effect.** Because winds tend to blow out of the east, they are called easterlies. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Polar easterlies:** The polar easterlies blow from polar high-pressure regions towards sub-polar lowpressure regions. It **blows from the northeast to the southwest direction in the northern** **hemisphere.** In the southern hemisphere, the direction of wind is from **southeast to northwest.** The Polar easterlies have characteristics of being extremely cold, dry and stable. **So, statement III is** **not correct.**
**Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Distribution of Continents and Oceans
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following parts of the continental
margins has the highest biological productivity?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Continental margins** are underwater edges of continents and consist of three main parts: **the** **continental shelves, continental slope, continental rise and deep-oceanic trenches.** Of these, the deep-oceanic trenches are the areas which are of considerable interest so far as the distribution of oceans and continents is concerned.
● The Continental margin consists of the following:
○ **Continental Shelf:** It is the continental margin which starts from the shoreline and extended upto the continental edge. The reasons for the formation of shelves are:
