■ Submergence of a part of a continent, ■ the relative rise in sea level, ■ sedimentary deposits, smaller continental shelves could have been caused by wave erosion
where the land is eroded by the sea. ○ Continental shelves covers an area of **7.5% of the total area of the oceans** and **18% of** **earth’s dry land area.** The average width of the shelf is 70 -80 kms. ○ Continental Shelves are economically and ecologically significant. It is one of the **most** **accessible part of the ocean.** Shelves provide conditions for the **growth of millions of** **plankton and microorganisms** through penetration of sunlight which makes them excellent breeding ground for fish. **Marine food** almost comes entirely from continental shelves. It is also the source of **Fossil fuels and leads to the formation of metallic and non metallic ores**. Hence it has the **highest biological productivity.** ○ **Continental Slope**: It descends from the edge of the continental shelf down to the deeper ocean floor. It is **steeper than the continental shelf.** ○ **Continental Rise:** It lies beyond the continental slope and consists of sediment that has cascaded down the slope. It is a more gradual incline that eventually merges with the abyssal plain. ○ **Deep-oceanic trenches:** These are the **long, narrow trenches that plunge as the great** **ocean deep** to a depth of 30,000 feet. Most of the deepest trenches are not located amid oceans. They are more often found close to the continents, particularly in the Pacific Ocean, where several deep trenches have been found.
**Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements is/are correct?
I. - Active plate margins are associated with
earthquakes and volcanism.
II. 	 Passive plate margins represent transitions
within a single tectonic plate.
III. 	 The East Pacific Rise is an example of a
passive plate margin.
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Seafloor spreading** is a geologic process in which tectonic plates—large slabs of Earth's lithosphere— split apart from each other. Seafloor spreading and other tectonic activity processes are the **result of** **mantle convection.** It occurs at divergent plate boundaries, along mid-ocean ridges—large mountain ranges rising from the ocean floor. Oceanic crust slowly moves away from mid-ocean ridges and sites of seafloor spreading. As it moves, it becomes cooler, denser and thicker. Eventually, older oceanic crust encounters a tectonic boundary with continental crust.
● In some cases, oceanic crust encounters an active plate margin. An **active plate margin** is an actual plate boundary, where **oceanic crust and continental crust crash with each other.**
● **Active plate margins are often the site of earthquakes and volcanoes.** Oceanic crust created by seafloor spreading, such as that along the **East Pacific Rise,** may become part of the Ring of Fire, a horseshoe-shaped pattern of **volcanoes and earthquake zones surrounding the Pacific Ocean** **basin. So, statement I is correct and statement III is not correct.**
● In other cases, oceanic crust encounters a passive plate margin. Passive margins are not plate boundaries, but areas where **a single tectonic plate transitions from oceanic lithosphere to continental** **lithosphere**. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Passive margins are not sites of faults or subduction zones**. Thick layers of sediment overlay the transitional crust of a passive margin. The oceanic crust of the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, for instance, will either become part of the passive margin on the North American plate (on the east coast of North America) or the Eurasian plate (on the west coast of Europe).
**Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
● Active continental margins tend to have narrow continental shelves.
● Passive continental margins are continental margins that are not tectonically active.
● These areas have flat lands and have a wide continental shelf, which is the submerged border of the continent.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Distribution of Continents and Oceans
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** Oceans in the northern hemisphere record relatively higher temperatures than in the southern hemisphere.
**Statement II:** In the northern hemisphere the land surface area is much larger than in the southern hemisphere.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is not correct
options.d: Statement I is not correct but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The temperature-depth profile for the ocean water shows how the temperature decreases with increasing depth.
● The **average surface temperature of ocean** water is around **27°C** and steadily **decreases from** **the equator toward the poles**, at an average rate of 0.5°C per degree of latitude. At 20° latitude, the temperature is about 22°C; at 40°, it drops to 14°C; and near the poles, it approaches 0°C.
● Oceans in the **Northern Hemisphere are generally warmer than those in the Southern** **Hemisphere.** Interestingly, the highest temperatures are not found exactly at the equator, but slightly north of it. This temperature variation, reflected in the average annual temperatures of 19°C in the Northern Hemisphere and 16°C in the Southern Hemisphere, is primarily due to the **unequal** **distribution of land and water between the two hemispheres.** Land **heats up and cools down** **more quickly and intensely than water.** The greater land area in the **Northern Hemisphere** **absorbs more solar radiation,** which warms both the land and the adjacent ocean waters. In contrast, the ocean-dominated Southern Hemisphere experiences more moderate temperatures due to the thermal buffering capacity of water. **So, statements I and II are correct.** **So, both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText: Despite being located at similar latitudes, the British Isles experience milder winters compared to the Northeast coast of Canada. What is the primary reason?

options.a: The British Isles lie at a lower elevation
options.b: The British Isles have longer daylight hours in winter
options.c: The British Isles are influenced by the warm North Atlantic Drift
options.d: The British Isles are frequently influenced by maritime westerlies

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Ocean currents** closely influence the distribution of temperature, pressure, winds and precipitation,
which directly or indirectly influence the economy and society of the people, especially those living in the coastal regions.
● Currents move from **warm temperature areas to colder temperature areas and vice versa.** As they move from one place to another they partly attempt to modify the temperature. The temperature of a mass of water affects the temperature of the air above it. Therefore, the ocean current that **moves from the equatorial region to the colder latitudes raises the temperature of the** **air** in the areas into which it moves.
○ For example, the warm **North Atlantic Drift flows northward along the western coast** **of Europe,** helping to keep regions like **Great Britain and Norway** largely ice-free during winter. The influence of this ocean current becomes evident when compared to the winter conditions of the **Northeast Coast of Canada,** which lies at a similar latitude. However, this Canadian coast is affected by the **cold Labrador Current,** causing it to remain ice-bound in winter. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - The ocean crust rocks are much younger than the continental rocks.
II. 	 Rocks closer to the mid-oceanic ridges have normal polarity.
III. 	 Mid-ocean ridges generally have shallowfocus earthquakes.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Concept of Seafloor spreading:** The post-drift studies provided considerable information that was not available at the time Wegener put forth his concept of continental drift. Particularly, the mapping of the ocean floor and palaeomagnetic studies of rocks from oceanic regions revealed the following facts:
○ It was realised that all along the mid-oceanic ridges, volcanic eruptions are common and they bring huge amounts of lava to the surface in this area. ○ The rocks equidistant on either side of the **crest of mid-oceanic ridges** show remarkable similarities in terms of period of formation, chemical compositions and magnetic properties. **Rocks closer to the mid-oceanic ridges** have **normal polarity and are the youngest**. The age of the rocks increases as one moves away from the crest. **So, statement II is correct.**
■ The hot magma at the ridge erupts as lava that forms new sea-floor. When the lava cools,
the **magnetite crystals** take on the then magnetic **polarity (normal/reversal)** and as more lava erupts, it pushes the sea-floor horizontally away from ridge axis. The magnetic polarity of Earth has witnessed many events of polarity reversals in the geological past. **During the normal polarity,** the **Earth’s magnetic north** coincides to **present-day** **geographic north pole** and in reversed polarity, the Earth magnetic south coincides to present-day geographic north pole. This gives a **continuous pattern of normal and** **reversal magnetic stripes across the sea-floor lying parallel to ocean ridge axis.** ○ The **ocean crust rocks are much younger than the continental rocks.** The age of rocks in the oceanic crust is nowhere more than 200 million years old. Some of the continental rock formations are as old as 3,200 million years. **So, statement I is correct.** ○ The sediments on the ocean floor are unexpectedly very thin. Scientists were expecting, if the ocean floors were as old as the continents, to have a complete sequence of sediments for a period of much longer duration. However, nowhere was the sediment column found to be older than 200 million years. ○ The **deep trenches** have **deep-seated** earthquake occurrences, while in the **mid-oceanic** **ridge areas**, the **quake foci have shallow depths. So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Movement of Ocean Water (Tides, Waves, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:**
The pattern of ocean currents in the northern
Indian Ocean shows a clear seasonal reversal
between summer and winter.
**Statement II:**
The Indian Ocean is enclosed by continental
masses in the north.
**Statement III:**
The Indian Ocean is entirely influenced by the
seasonal changes of the monsoon winds.
Which one of the following is correct in
respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement II and Statement III are
correct and both of them explain Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement II and Statement III are
correct, but only one of them explains
Statement I
options.c:
Only one of the Statements II and III is
correct and that explains Statement I
options.d:
Neither Statement II nor Statement III is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Ocean currents are the continuous, predictable, directional movement of seawater driven by gravity, wind (Coriolis Effect) and water density.
● **Currents of the Indian Ocean:** The pattern of circulation of currents in the Indian Ocean differs from the general pattern of circulation in the **Atlantic Ocean and the Pacific Ocean.** This is because the Indian Ocean is **blocked by the** **continental masses in the north.** The general pattern of circulation in the southern section of the Indian Ocean is anti-clockwise as is that of other oceans. But in the northern section, there is a **clear reversal of currents in winter and summer.** These are **completely under the influence of the seasonal changes of the monsoon winds.** So there is a clear reversal of currents in the winter and summer season, i.e/southwestwards during the north-east Monsoon, north-eastwards during the southwest Monsoon and variable during the transition season.
**So, both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain Statement I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Places in News and Geopolitical Hotspots (Ukraine, Taiwan Strait, Hormuz, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the composition of the Earth's
crust, consider the following statements:
I. - Oxygen occupies more than 90% of the
Earth's crust by volume.
II. 	 Iron is the second most abundant element by
volume in the Earth's crust.
III. 	 Silicon is the third most abundant element in
the Earth's crust by weight.
How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Earth is composed of various kinds of elements. These elements are in solid form in the outer layer of the Earth and in hot and molten form in the interior.
● **Oxygen** is the most abundant element in the **Earth’s** **crust** **by** **volume**, accounting for over **90%**. This is because oxygen atoms are large and form part of most minerals, especially in the form of oxides and silicates. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● **Silicon** is the second most abundant element **by volume (0.88%)**, commonly found as part of silicate minerals such as quartz, feldspar and mica. **So, statement II is not correct.**
○ **Iron** accounts for **0.49% by volume** in the Earths crust.
● **Aluminium** is the **third most abundant element by weight** in the Earth’s crust, after oxygen and silicon. It is primarily found in minerals like bauxite and feldspar. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** Dark-coloured soils promote quicker seed germination and more rapid early plant growth.
**Statement II:** Dark-coloured soils absorb more solar energy than light-coloured soils.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is not correct
options.d: Statement I is not correct but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown: ● **Soil** is a thin layer made up of **broken rock particles, organic matter, water and air** that covers most of the land. Soil is essential for life on Earth and is one of our most valuable natural resources. It supports life and also needs living organisms to form. All soils are made from four main parts: **air, water,** **organic matter and minerals.** Different soils have different proportions of these parts, which shape their nature and potential uses. **Dark-coloured soils** get their colour from a **high content of organic** **matter**. They **absorb more solar radiation than light-coloured soils**, allowing them to warm up faster. This faster warming promotes **quicker seed germination** and **more rapid early plant** **growth.** **So, statements I and II are correct.** **So, both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText:
Which of the following are the major characteristics of metamorphic rocks?
I. - These are denser and harder than the original parent rock.
II. 	 These contain an abundant amount of fossils.
III. 	 These contain well-developed foliated or lineated structures.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The primary **igneous and secondary sedimentary rocks** may change their physical and chemical characteristics and form due to the **huge pressure and heat.** The transformed tertiary rocks are called **metamorphic rocks.**
● Metamorphism makes the rocks go through the process of **re-crystallization** which results in the **transformation of the Protolith** into harder and denser rock. Re-crystallization destroys bedding planes and joints systems. As a result, the **marble or quartz** may show no stratifications in the rocks. The metamorphic rocks cover a significant part of the earth's crust.
● The major characteristics of metamorphic rocks are as follows: ○ metamorphic rocks are **denser and harder than the original parent rock.** **So, statement** **I is correct.** ○ the re-crystallization process makes bigger crystals which are prominent ○ the ores are segregated that makes it easy to mine ○ These **rocks do not contain fossils. So, statement II is not correct.** ○ They may contain **well developed foliated or lineated structures.** **So, statement III is** **correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText: What type of processes are related to the dissolution of soluble rocks, such as limestone and gypsum in morphogenetic regions?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Any **limestone or dolomitic region** showing typical landforms produced by the action of groundwater through the **processes of solution and deposition** is called **Karst topography,** after the typical topography developed in limestone rocks of the Karst region in the Balkans adjacent to the Adriatic Sea. The karst topography is characterised by erosional and depositional landforms.
● Karst regions have a bleak landscape, occasionally broken by precipitous slopes. There is a **general** **absence of surface drainage,** as most of the surface water has gone underground. Streams rising on other rocks only flow over limestone for a short distance and then disappear underground.
● Processes related to the **dissolution of soluble rocks,** such as **limestone and gypsum**, by water are known as **karst processes.** These processes contribute to the formation of distinctive landforms, including sinkholes, caves and underground drainage systems, in **morphogenetic regions** with soluble bedrock.
**Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
With reference to the sand dunes, consider the following statements:
I. - Barchans are crescent-shaped dunes formed by wind from one direction.
II. 	 Transverse dunes are asymmetrical in shape and form where winds blow from a constant direction.
III. 	 Seif are long, narrow ridges that are parallel to the direction of prevailing winds.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Sand dunes:** A Landform is created by the movement of sand by wind. The dunes are like small mountains, ridges or hills. Sand dunes are a very prominent feature of desert areas. The coarser materials are deposited in drifts in the shape of crests or definite summits or hills or ridges, called dunes. An ideal dune has a long windward slope rising to a crest and a much steeper leeward slope. A sand dune may be defined as a mound or ridge of **windblown sand, rising to various** **heights up to 50 m.** It is found in hot deserts and above the high-water mark on low-lying coasts where sand is constantly renewed by onshore winds blowing across the sandy beaches.
● **Barchan:** They are **crescent (half-moon) or arc-shaped,** appear convex in shape and are **primarily formed by wind from one** **direction.** They are found in sandy deserts all over the world. Two “horns” face downwind on this type of dune, with the steeper slope known as the slip face facing away from the wind. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● **Transverse dunes:** These dunes are **asymmetrical in shape** and form where light to moderate winds blow **from a constant direction.** These dunes take the shape of a series of crests and troughs whose peaks are perpendicular to the direction of prevailing winds. These dunes appear like sea waves. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Seif or longitudinal dunes:** They are long, narrow ridges that are **parallel to the direction of** **prevailing winds.** The winds come from different directions. The winds blow straight along the corridors between the lines of sand dunes and sweep the corridors clear of sand. Such types are commonly seen in the Sahara Desert, North Africa, Arabia and can also be seen in the western part of the Thar Desert of India. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Environment
questions.microTopic: Climate Change and Impacts of Climate Change over Earth
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Stalactites
II. 	 Stalagmites
III. 	 Sinkholes
IV. 	 Lapies
V. 	 Limestone pavements
How many of the above are depositional landforms?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Depositional Landforms:** Many depositional forms develop within the **limestone caves.** The chief chemical in limestone is calcium carbonate which is easily soluble in carbonated water **(carbon** **dioxide** **absorbed** **rainwater)**. This calcium carbonate is deposited when the water carrying it in solution evaporates or loses its carbon dioxide as it trickles over rough rock surfaces.
● **Stalactites** hang as icicles of different diameters. Normally they are broad at their bases and taper towards the free ends showing up in a variety of forms. **Stalagmites** rise up from the **floor of the caves.** Stalagmites form **due to dripping water from** **the surface** or through the **thin pipe of the stalactite**, immediately below it.
● Stalagmites may take the shape of a column, a disc, with either a smooth, rounded bulging end or a **miniature crater-like depression.** The stalagmites and stalactites eventually fuse to give rise to columns and pillars of different diameters. **So, points I and II are correct.**
● **Erosional landforms:** ○ **Pools, Sinkholes, Lapies and Limestone Pavements:** Small to medium-sized round to subrounded shallow depressions called **swallow holes** form on the surface of limestones through solution. **Sinkholes** are very common in **limestone/ karst areas.** A sinkhole is an opening more or less circular at the top and funnel-shaped towards the bottom with sizes varying in area from a few sq. m to a hectare and with depth from less than half a metre to thirty metres or more. ○ When sinkholes and dolines join together because of slumping of materials along their margins or due to roof collapse of caves, long, narrow to wide trenches called valley **sinks or Uvalas** **form.** Gradually, most of the surface of the limestone is eaten away by these pits and trenches, leaving it **extremely irregular with a maze of points, grooves** and **ridges or lapies.** Especially, these ridges or lapies form due to differential solution activity along parallel to subparallel joints. The lapie field may eventually turn into somewhat **smooth limestone** **pavements. So, points III, IV and V are not correct.**
**So, only two of the above are depositional landforms.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText: With reference to the Earth’s internal structure, Repiti Discontinuity is a transition zone between:

options.a: Upper mantle and the lower mantle

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Earth's mantle,** which lies between the crust and the core, is primarily divided into two main layers: the **upper mantle and the lower** **mantle**. The boundary that separates these two layers is known as the **Repetti Discontinuity.**
● **Upper Mantle:** ○ Though mostly solid, parts of the upper mantle are ductile (more malleable), which plays a crucial role in tectonic activity. ○ It extends to a depth of about 410 kilometers. ○ The upper mantle is further subdivided into: ■ **Lithosphere:** The rigid outer layer that includes the uppermost mantle and the crust. ■ **Asthenosphere:** The underlying, semi-fluid layer that allows the lithosphere to move.
● **Transition Zone:** It is located between **410 km and 660 km depth**, this zone acts as a boundary within the mantle.It restricts the direct exchange of materials between the upper and lower mantle.
● A key feature of this zone is its **high water content**—minerals here can hold as much water as is found in all of Earth’s surface oceans, stored within the crystal structures.
● **Lower Mantle:** ○ The lower mantle lies beneath the transition zone, stretching from about 660 km to 2,700 km in depth. ○ It is denser and hotter than the upper mantle and behaves more rigidly, though it can still flow very slowly over long timescales. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Conrad Discontinuity:** It is a transition zone between **SIAL (Silica and** **aluminum) and SIMA** (silica, iron, and magnesium). It is considered to be the boundary between the **upper and lower continental crusts.**
● The density of the **crust at the surface is 2.7 g/ cm3 and at the bottom limit, it** **is 2.9 g/cm3.** The demarcating limit is known as **Mohorovicic (Moho)** **discontinuity from where mantle starts.**
● The core of the Earth lies at an average depth of 2890 km from the surface of the Earth. It is separated from the mantle by the **Gutenberg discontinuity**.

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Insurance and Pension
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the floodplain landforms:
I. - Natural levees are low, linear ridges of coarse deposits found along the banks of large rivers.
II. 	 Point bars are formed on the concave side of meanders due to sediment deposition by flowing water.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Deposition develops a floodplain just as erosion makes valleys. A **floodplain is a major landform of river deposition.** Large-sized materials are deposited first when the stream channel breaks into a gentle slope. Thus, normally, fine-sized materials like **sand, silt and clay** are carried by relatively slow-moving waters in gentler channels usually found in the plains and deposited over the bed and when the waters spill over the banks during flooding above the bed.
● The flood deposits of spilled waters carry relatively finer materials like silt and clay. The floodplains in a delta are called **delta plains.**
● Natural levees and point bars are some of the important landforms found associated with floodplains. **Natural levees** are found along the banks of large rivers. They are **low, linear and parallel ridges of** **coarse deposits** along the banks of rivers, quite often cut into individual mounds. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Point bars** are also known as meander bars. They are found on the **concave side of meanders of** **large rivers** and are sediments deposited linearly by flowing waters along the bank. They are almost uniform in profile and in width and contain mixed sizes of sediments. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Sheltered and tideless coastline
II. 	 Shallow sea water adjoining the delta
III. 	 Presence of strong tidal currents at the river mouth
IV. 	 Active lateral and vertical erosion in the upper course of the river
How many of the above are favorable conditions for the formation of a delta?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Delta** is a triangular feature with its **apex pointing up stream** and has a **fan shaped area** of fine alluvium. The world‘s largest delta is made by river Ganga and river Brahmaputra known as Sundarban delta.
● Favourable conditions for the formation of Deltas: ○ There should be **active lateral and vertical** **erosion** in the upper course of the river to provide large amounts of sediments. **So, point IV** **is correct.** ○ **Shallow sea water** adjoining the delta. **So,** **point II is correct.** ○ **Tideless and sheltered coast. So, point I is** **correct.** ○ There should be **no strong current at the** **river mouth** which may wash away the sediments. **So, point III is not correct.** **So, only three of the above are favorable conditions for** **the formation of a delta.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Glacier landform Description
I. - Aretes A shallow pass between two high points
II. - Horns A pyramidal peak formed by erosion
III. Cirques A bowl-shaped depression at a glacier head
IV. Crevasses A deep crack in the glacier surface
How many of the above pairs are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Masses of ice moving as sheets over the land or as linear flows down the slopes of mountains in broad **trough-like valleys (mountain and valley glaciers)** are called glaciers. The movement of glaciers is slow unlike water flow. The movement could be a few centimetres to a few metres a day or even less or more. **Types of landforms produced by Glaciers:**
● **Aretes, Cols and Horns: Aretes** are **saw-** **toothed ridges** in which there are multiple ridges in series and rows and they **divide two** **cirques.** Cols are **shallow passes between** **two high points of the mountain.** They have sharp edge passes or saddles between two adjacent cirques. **Horn** is **pyramid shaped** **mountain peaks** which is formed by erosion on three sides **by 3-4 cirques** on each side of the peak. **Example:** Matterhorn in Switzerland. **So,** **pair I is not correctly matched and pair II is** **correctly matched.**
● **Cirques or corrie:** Snow accumulates at the upper end of arm-chaired or **bowlshaped depressions** **formed at the head of the glacial valley are called cirques.** The structure of the cirque has steep walls on three sides with one side open on the valley front. The cirques are like a cradle of the glacier where it is formed by plucking rock and intense freezing and thawing (causing frost shattering) from the side. It makes steep walls and scoop out at the base of the glacier in which snow accumulates and ice formations flowing away from the cirque. In certain areas the deepest part of these cirque hollows is filled with water called cirque lakes or tarn. **So,** **pair III is correctly matched.**
● **Crevasses: These are deep, vertical cracks** **or fractures that occur on the surface of a** **glacier** due to the differential movement of glacier ice. These typically form in the upper, brittle layer of a glacier when the ice flows over uneven terrain or accelerates**. So, pair IV is** **correctly matched.** **So, only three of the above pairs are correctly** **matched.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer**.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements regarding fold
mountains and block mountains:
I. - Fold mountains are formed due to folding
of crustal rocks while block mountains are
the result of faulting caused by tensile and
compressive forces.
II. 	 Andes mountains are examples of block
mountains while black forest mountains are
examples of fold mountains.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **a**
**Explanation:**
● **Fold Mountains:** Folded mountains are **formed due to folding of crustal rocks** by compressive forces generated by endogenetic forces coming from within the earth. These are the highest and most extensive mountains of the world and are found in all the continents. **Rockies, Andes, Alps,** **Himalayas, Atlas etc. are the examples of folded mountains.**
● **Block Mountains:** Block mountains are **the result of faulting caused by tensile and compressive** **forces** motored by endogenetic forces coming from within the earth. Block mountains represent the upstanding parts of the ground between two faults or on either side of a rift valley or a graben. Essentially, block mountains are formed due to faulting in the ground surface. They are also called horst mountains. **The Vosges in France, Black Forest Mountains in Germany are the typical examples of** **block mountains. So, statement I is correct and statement II is not correct.**
**Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water on Earth Surface, Ocean Relief
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Type of Mountain Example
I. - Residual Mount Kilimanjaro in Tanzania
II. - Volcanic Fuji Yama in Japan
III. - Dome Satpura Hills in India
How many of the above pairs are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Mountains are significant relief features of the second order on the Earth’s surface. A mountain may have several forms.
● **Residual mountains:** They are formed through the **prolonged erosion of elevated landforms,** leaving behind resistant remnants. These are also referred to as mountains of denudation. Notable examples include the **Scottish Highlands,** the **Scandinavian Mountains** and the mountainous regions of the Balkan Peninsula. **So, pair I is not correctly matched.**
○ **Mount Kilimanjaro** in Tanzania is primarily a **stratovolcano** formed by the accumulation of volcanic ash, lava, and rock. It is considered a dormant volcano.
● **Volcanic Mountains:** When tectonic plates move about, volcanoes are formed and when volcanoes erupt, mountains are formed in turn. A shield volcano has a gently sloping cone due to the low viscosity of the emitted material, primarily basalt. Most volcanic mountains occur in a band surrounding the Pacific Ocean. Collectively, they are known as the **Pacific Ring of Fire.** The famous volcanic mountains are stratovolcanoes or shield volcanoes. Mount Mauna Loa in Hawaii Islands, Mount Popa in Myanmar, Vesuvius in Italy, Cotopaxi in Equador and **Fuji Yama in Japan** are examples of volcanic mountains. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● **Dome mountains:** They are formed when **large globs** **of magma float up from beneath the crust and** **push up surface rocks,** creating a rounded swelling in the crust. Once the magma cools, it creates a large dome of harder rock under the surface, which erosion sometimes reveals. Examples include the **Black Hills of** **South Dakota and the Adirondack Mountains of** **New York. So, pair III is not correctly matched.**
○ **Satpura** and Vindhya mountains in the centralwestern part of India are considered block mountains. **So, only one of the above pairs is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer**.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Distribution of Continents and Oceans
questions.questionText:
According to the Continental Drift Theory by
Alfred Wegener, which forces were responsible
for the formation of continents?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In 1912, **German meteorologist Alfred Wegener** proposed the **Continental Drift Theory,** offering a comprehensive explanation for the distribution of continents and oceans. According to Wegener, all present-day continents were once part of a single massive landmass called **Pangaea,** meaning “all Earth,” which was surrounded by a vast ocean named Panthalassa, meaning “all water.”
● Wegener suggested that around 200 million years ago, Pangaea began to break apart. It first split into two large landmasses: Laurasia in the north and Gondwanaland in the south. Over time, these landmasses further fragmented to form the continents as we know them today.
● To support his theory, **Wegener presented multiple lines of evidence**, including matching geological structures, fossil records and climatic similarities across continents. He also proposed the **mechanisms behind the drift: the pole-fleeing force—arising from the Earth’s rotation**—and the **tidal force, generated by the gravitational pull of the Moon and the Sun.** Wegener believed that, although weak, these forces could cause significant continental movement over millions of years. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Places in News and Geopolitical Hotspots (Ukraine, Taiwan Strait, Hormuz, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following information:
Mineral Largest producer Characteristic
I. - Amphibole Rajasthan Crystallize in igneous and metamorphic rocks
II. - Quartz Arunachal Hard mineral virtually insoluble in water
III. Feldspar Gujarat Used in ceramics and glass making
IV. Mica Andhra pradesh Resistant to heat and sudden change in temperature In how many of the above rows is the given information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Minerals** are naturally occurring inorganic substances with definite chemical compositions and physical properties. Each mineral has specific characteristics—such as color, composition, crystal structure and industrial use—that help in their identification and classification. **Some Major minerals and their characteristics:**
● **Amphibole:** Aluminium, calcium, silica, iron, magnesium are the major elements of amphiboles. They form **7 per cent of the Earth’s crust.** It is green or black in colour and is used in the **asbestos** **industry.** Hornblende is another form of amphibole. Amphiboles can **crystallize in igneous and** **metamorphic rocks** with a wide range of bulk chemistries.
○ Out of the total resources, **Rajasthan has a major share of 13.62 million tonnes (59.44%)**, followed by Karnataka 8.28 million tonnes (36.16%), Andhra Pradesh 0.79 million tonnes (3.48%), Jharkhand 0.15 million tonnes (0.68%) and remaining a meager 0.06 million tonnes are distributed in the states of Odisha and Uttarakhand. **So, row I is correctly matched.**
● **Quartz:** It is one of the most important components of sand and granite. It consists of silica. **It is a** **hard mineral virtually insoluble in water**. It is white or colourless and used in radio and radar. It is one of the most important components of granite.
○ **Rajasthan occupies a unique position for being the largest producer of quartz in India** and contributing to a large extent to the total production in the country. As a mineral-rich state, Rajasthan has vast deposits of high-grade quartz resources. The state has an excellent geological base, with deposits distributed throughout the regions of the state. **So, row II is not correctly** **matched.**
● **Feldspar:** Silicon and oxygen are common elements in all types of feldspar and sodium potassium, calcium, aluminium etc. are found in specific feldspar varieties. Half of the Earth’s crust is composed of feldspar. It has light cream to salmon pink colour. It is **used in ceramics and glass making.**
○ **Rajasthan a major producer of Feldspar, producing 87.94 mn tonnes of it which is** **used in ceramics and glass**. Bhilwara and Ajmer is the leading producer of glass grade Feldspar. **So, row III is not correctly matched.**
● **Mica:** It comprises **potassium, aluminium, magnesium, iron, silica etc**. It forms 4 per cent of the earth’s crust. It is commonly found in igneous and metamorphic rocks. It is used in electrical instruments. It possesses highly perfect basal cleavage due to which it can easily and accurately split into very thin sheets or films of any specified thickness. It has a unique combination of elasticity, toughness, flexibility and transparency. It **possesses resistance to heat and sudden change in temperature** and high dielectric strength. It is chemically inert, stable and does not absorb water**.**
○ Andhra Pradesh is the largest producer of Mica. Nellore district of Andhra Pradesh is famous for its mica (crude) production.  **So, row IV is correctly matched.** **So, in only two of the above rows the given information is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer**.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText: Loess, Yardangs and Blow outs are the depositional and erosional features of:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Winds** are the most widespread geomorphic agents in the **deserts, arid and semi-arid regions.** Wind erosion is effective only up to **180 cm above the ground surface.** Maximum wind erosion occurs at short distances. Unlike rivers and glaciers, winds erode the rocks from all sides because of their variable directions.
● **Loess:** It is primarily located close to the **desert floor or crust** and is a feature made by **deposition.** The word originated from the German word Loess which means **loose or unconsolidated**. The particles are silt-sized and yellow in colour. The deposits are very thick and have homogeneous, unstratified silt deposits. The loess areas are very soft, so when roads are constructed in these areas witness frequent sinking of the soil and the roads also sink with it.
● Some of the **erosional landforms** of the wind are: ○ **Yardangs:** These are **elongated ridges** formed by wind oriented with the **prevailing winds**. These are larger hill-sized features sculpted by the wind. Yardangs are composed of cohesive silts and clays, sandstone, or limestone. They develop in regions with strong unidirectional winds. ○ **Blow Outs or deflation basins:** Rocks that are exposed to the sand blasting of prevailing winds become pitted, grooved and polished. Deflation basins, called blowouts, **are hollows formed** **by the removal of particles by wind**. Sand grains are rolled and the entire bed is lowered, in some cases up to one metre, resulting in shallow depressions called blowouts. This depression may be a few metres to as much as a kilometre across.
**Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Mushroom rock:** When the composition of rock is mixed, where there are alternate layers of soft and hard rock, the rock is subjected to wind abrasion with differential erosion. More sediment load is carried by wind at lower heights, **so erosion is more** **at the base or near the base of the rock.** The upper blowing winds carry less sediment load as it becomes heavy to carry so erosion at the upper part of the rock is less. This gives the mushroom rock its famous shape of a **top and base thin.** These rocks resemble rock pillars shaped like mushrooms. Such types of formations can be seen in the **Sahara Desert in Africa** and the **Thar Desert in India.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Literature of Ancient India
questions.questionText:
Which of the following factors contributes to the lower frequency of landslides in the Western Ghats as compared to the Himalayas?
I. - Western Ghats are geologically older and more tectonically stable than the Himalayas.
II. 	 Seismic activity is significantly lower in the Western Ghats than Himalayan region.
III. 	 Western Ghats generally have more gradual slopes than the Himalayas.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Landslides:** These are relatively rapid and perceptible movements. The materials involved are relatively dry. The size and shape of the detached mass depend on the nature of **discontinuities in the rock,** the **degree of weathering** and the **steepness of the slope**.
● Landslides are more frequent in the Himalayan region when compared with the Western Ghats. It is because of the following reasons:
○ The **Western Ghats** are part of the ancient Peninsular Plateau, which has undergone long periods of **weathering and stabilisation**. As a result, they are **structurally more stable and** **less prone to sudden slope failures**. In contrast, the Himalayas are geologically young and still adjusting to tectonic forces, making them structurally weak and more susceptible to landslides. **So, statement I is correct.** ○ The **Himalayas** are formed by the collision of the Indian and Eurasian plates, a process that is still ongoing. This **tectonic activity causes frequent earthquakes**, uplift and crustal movements, all of which destabilise slopes and trigger landslides. The **Western Ghats, being** **tectonically inactive**, do not experience such constant structural disturbances. **So,** **statement II is correct.** ○ Topography plays a crucial role in landslide formation. **The Himalayas have extremely steep** **and rugged slopes,** which, when combined with rainfall, tectonic activity, or deforestation, easily fail. The **Western Ghats have gentler, more rounded slopes**, reducing the gravitational pull on soil and rocks and thereby lowering the risk of landslides. **So, statement** **III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Origin, Evolution, Geological Time Scale
questions.questionText: Pediments are best described as:

options.a: Steep ridges formed by glacial erosion
options.b: Flat plains formed by river deposition
options.c:
Gently sloping rock surfaces at the base of
mountains
options.d: Isolated hills rising abruptly from plains

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Pediments:** Landscape evolution in deserts is primarily concerned with the formation and extension of pediments. These are **gently sloping terrains** found at the foot of the mountains over a solid **rock** **base formed due to abrasion.** They are the junction that occurs between the zone of a steep erosional slope and the depositional lowlands. Such rocky floors form through the erosion of the mountain front through a combination of lateral erosion by streams and sheet flooding.
● Erosion starts along the **steep margins of the landmass or the steep sides of the tectonically** **controlled steep incision** features over the landmass. Once pediments are formed with a steep wash slope followed by a cliff or free face above it, the steep wash slope and free face retreat backwards. This method of erosion is termed as parallel retreat of slopes through backwasting. So, through **parallel** **retreat of slopes, the pediments extend backwards** at the expense of the mountain front and gradually, the mountain gets reduced, leaving an **inselberg,** which is a remnant of the mountain. That’s how the high relief in desert areas is reduced to low featureless plains called **pediplains.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer**.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Tectonic Plate Location
I. - Scotia Between the South American Plate and the Antarctic Plate
II. - Caroline Between the Philippine plate and Indian plate
III. - Cocos Between the African Plate and the Eurasian Plate
How many of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A tectonic plate (also called a **lithospheric plate**) is a massive, irregularly-shaped slab of solid rock, generally composed of both **continental and oceanic lithosphere**. Plates move horizontally over the asthenosphere as rigid units.
● The theory of plate tectonics proposes that the Earth’s lithosphere is divided into seven major and **some** **minor plates.**
● The following are some of the important minor plates: ○ **Scotia Plate**: Between the **South American Plate and the Antarctic Plate. So, pair I is** **correctly matched.** ○ **Caroline plate:** Between the **Philippine and Indian plate** (North of New Guinea). **So, pair** **II is correctly matched.** ○ **Cocos plate:** Between **Central America and the Pacific plate. So, pair III is not** **correctly matched.** ○ Nazca plate: Between South America and the Pacific plate ○ Arabian plate: Mostly the Saudi Arabian landmass ○ Philippine plate: Between the Asiatic and Pacific plate ○ Fuji plate: North-east of Australia.
**So, only two of the pairs given above are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer**.
**Knowledge Box**
The **major plates** are as follows:
● Antarctica and the surrounding oceanic plate
● North American (with western Atlantic floor separated from the South American plate along the Caribbean islands) plate
● South American (with western Atlantic floor separated from the North American plate along the Caribbean islands) plate
● Pacific plate
● India-Australia-New Zealand plate
● Africa with the eastern Atlantic floor plate
● Eurasia and the adjacent oceanic plate.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Minerals and Resources (Coal, Iron, Bauxite, Lead, Zinc, Pyrites, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - Acid lava is rich in metallic minerals while
basic lava is rich in silica.
II. 	 Unlike acid lavas, basic lavas are highly
viscous with a high melting point.
III. 	 The Acid lava volcanoes have a steeper slope
than basic lava.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The magma is heavily charged with gases such as **carbon dioxide, sulphurated hydrogen** and small proportions of **nitrogen, chlorine and other volatile substances.** The gases and vapour increase the mobility and explosiveness of the lavas which are emitted through the orifice or vent of a volcano during a volcanic eruption. There are two main types of lava.
● **Basic lavas:** These are the **hottest lavas and are highly fluid.** They are dark coloured like basalt, **rich in iron and magnesium but poor in silica**. As they are poured out of the volcano, they flow quietly and are not very explosive. Due to their high fluidity, they flow readily with a speed of 10 to 30 miles per hour. Since the **basic lava is rich in metallic minerals** and has a low melting point, it has greater fluidity. In this type of eruption, lava flows far and wide quietly with greater speed and spreads out in thin sheets over a large area. Thus, it leads to the formation of shields and lava domes.
● **Acid lavas:** These lavas **are highly viscous with a high melting point.** They are light-coloured, of low density and have a **high percentage of silica.** They flow slowly and seldom travel far before solidifying. The resultant cone is therefore — The rapid congealing of lava in the vent obstructs the flow of the outpouring lava, resulting in loud explosions, throwing out many volcanic bombs or pyroclasts. Sometimes the lavas are so viscous that they form a spine or plug at the crater like that of Mt. Pelee in Martinique. Some spines are very resistant and while most of the material of very old volcanoes is removed by erosion, the spine may remain, e.g. Puy de Dome. France. **So, statements I and II are** **not correct.**
● Contrary to basic lava, acid lava is rich in silica and has a relatively high melting point. Therefore, it is highly viscous and solidifies quickly. Hence, the acid lava volcanoes cause the formation of **usually higher** **land features with steeper slopes**. Acid lava cones have **steeper slopes than basic lava shields.** **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to the types of soils:
I. - Red soils contain a high amount of manganese dioxides.
II. 	 Red soils usually exhibit good drainage and aeration properties.
III. 	 Yellow soils have high amounts of organic matter.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Soil colour is the physical property of soil. It varies widely depending on the nature of parent material, organic matter content, climate, drainage conditions and aeration.
● The **red colour of a soil** is due to the **presence of iron compounds or manganese dioxides.** Since **unhydrated iron oxides are relatively unstable under moist conditions** and cannot exist in poorly drained soil, red colour usually indicates good drainage and aeration. **Well-developed red** **coloured soil has good drainage and aeration.** Such soils are relatively old or at least the soil material has been subjected to relatively intense weathering for a long period. **So, statements I and** **II are correct.**
● **Well-drained yellow soils have small amounts of organic matter** and other colouring material such as iron oxides, mixed up with large amounts of whitish sand. Other factors being the same, yellow colour is more common than red colour in regions of high humidity. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Places in News and Geopolitical Hotspots (Ukraine, Taiwan Strait, Hormuz, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - All lines of latitude, except the equator, progressively decrease in length as they move from the equator towards the poles.
II. 	 Latitudes always run in the west-east direction.
III. 	 The distance between any two longitudes is maximum at the equator and gradually decreases towards the poles.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Latitude:** It is the **angular distance of a point on the Earth’s surface**, measured in degrees from the centre of the Earth. It is parallel to a line, the equator, which lies midway between the poles. These lines are therefore called **parallels of latitude** and on a globe are actually circles, becoming smaller polewards. The equator represents 0° and the North and South Poles are 90°N. and 90°S.
● **All the parallels** or latitudes **except the equator** go on becoming **smaller towards the poles,** where each pole in reality is no more than a mere point. **Latitudes always run in the east-west** **direction.** Parallels of latitude intersect the longitudes at right angles. Many parallels of latitude can be drawn on a globe. Hence, any point on the globe is located on one of the other latitudes. **So, statement** **I is correct and statement II is not correct.**
● **Longitude:** It is an angular distance, measured in degrees along the **equator east or west of the** **Prime (or First) Meridian**. On the globe, longitude is shown as a series of semi-circles that run from pole to pole passing through the equator. Such lines are also called meridians. Unlike the equator which is centrally placed between the poles, any meridian could have been taken to begin the numbering of longitude. The actual distance between any **two consecutive longitudes** is the largest at the equator and it goes on **decreasing till it becomes zero at the Poles. So, statement III is correct.**
**Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - A high-altitude weather balloon to study ozone
II. 	 A jet aircraft on a transcontinental flight
III. 	 Burning up of meteors before reaching Earth
IV. 	 Formation of cumulonimbus clouds during a thunderstorm
How many of the above activities take place in the stratosphere?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The atmosphere consists of different layers with **varying density and temperature.** Density is highest near the surface of the earth and **decreases with increasing altitude**. The column of atmosphere is divided into five different layers depending upon the temperature condition. They are: **troposphere,** **stratosphere, mesosphere, thermosphere and exosphere.**
● The stratosphere is found above the tropopause and **extends up to a height of 50 km.** One important feature of the stratosphere is that it contains the ozone layer. This layer absorbs ultra-violet radiation and shields life on the earth from intense, harmful form of energy.
● Because of its **stability and low turbulence**, this layer is ideal for certain types of aircraft and scientific observations:
○ **A high-altitude weather balloon to study ozone:** These balloons are launched to monitor atmospheric composition and often reach the ozone layer located in the stratosphere. **So, point** **I is correct.** ○ **A jet aircraft on a transcontinental flight:** Commercial jets typically cruise at altitudes of 10–12 km, which is at the lower edge of the stratosphere. **So, point II is correct.**
● **Burning up of meteors before reaching Earth:** Most meteors disintegrate due to friction in the **mesosphere**, located between 50–85 km altitude. **So, point III is not correct.**
● **Formation of cumulonimbus clouds during a thunderstorm:** Weather phenomena including thunderstorms and cloud formation occur in the **troposphere**, the lowest atmospheric layer. **So, point** **IV is not correct.** **So, only two of the above activities take place in the stratosphere.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements about
thunderstorms:
I. - They are always produced by a nimbostratus
cloud.
II. 	 They are characterised by an intense updraft
of rising warm air.
III. 	 They are always accompanied by intense
lightning and thunder.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● As the winter season transforms into the spring season, the temperature rises, giving rise to thunderstorms, which are hazardous. **Thunderstorms have been defined as storms produced by** **a cumulonimbus cloud** and are **always accompanied by intense lightning and thunder.** Such storms are also called electrical storms or thunder showers. **So, statement I is not correct and** **statement III is correct.**
● It is also **characterised by intense updraft of rising warm air, which causes the clouds to grow** **bigger and rise to greater height**. This causes precipitation. Later, downdraft brings the cool air and the rain. **So, statement II is correct.**
● Severe storms, dust clouds and volcanic eruptions can also generate lightning. Thunderstorms are usually of short duration and seldom last over 2 hours. They are always accompanied by **strong wind gusts,** **heavy rain and sometimes hail.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water on Earth Surface, Ocean Relief
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following terms refers to
the average rate of temperature decrease with
increasing altitude in the troposphere?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Relief features such as **mountains, plateaus and plains** control the temperature by way of modifying its distribution. Mountains act as barriers against the movement of winds. The Himalayan ranges prevent cold winds of Central Asia from entering India during winter. Because of this Kolkata is not as cold as Guangzhou (Canton) in winter, though both are situated almost on the same latitude. As we **move** **upwards from sea level,** we experience a **gradual decrease in temperature.** Temperature decreases at **an average rate of 6****0****C per 1000 m. altitude**. It is known as the **normal lapse rate**.
● The air at lower elevations is warmer than that of higher elevations because it is closer to the heated surface of the Earth. As a result, mountains are cooler than the plains even during summer. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● A **thermal gradient** refers to the rate of change of temperature with distance. It essentially indicates how quickly the temperature rises or falls in a particular direction.
● The **adiabatic lapse rate** refers to the rate at which the temperature of an air parcel changes as it rises or sinks in the atmosphere without exchanging heat with its surroundings

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Insurance and Pension
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - The insolation received by the Earth is in
short waves forms.
II. 	 The Earth, like all other hot objects, radiates
heat energy received from Sun.
III. 	 More than 50 units of the total heat received
by the Earth is reflected back by clouds.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Heat budget of the Earth:**
● The insolation is made up of energy transmitted directly through the atmosphere and scattered energy**.** The insolation received by the **Earth is in short waves** and heats up its surface. The **Earth after** **being heated itself becomes a radiating body** and it **radiates energy to the atmosphere in** **long wave form.** This energy heats up the atmosphere from below. This process is known as terrestrial radiation. **So, statements I and II are correct.**
● The total heat (incoming solar radiation) received at the **top of the atmosphere is 100 units.** Roughly 35 units of it are reflected back into space even before reaching the surface of the Earth. Out of **these** **35 units**, 6 units are reflected back to space from the top of the atmosphere, **27 units are reflected** **by clouds** and 2 units from the **snow and ice-covered surfaces. So, statement III is not correct.**
● Out of the remaining 65 units (100-35), only 51 units reach the Earth’s surface and 14 units are absorbed by the **various gases, dust particles and water vapour of the atmosphere**. The Earth in turn, radiates back 51 units in the form of terrestrial radiation. Out of these 51 units of terrestrial radiation, 34 units are absorbed by the atmosphere and the remaining 17 units directly go to space. The atmosphere also radiates **48 units (14 units of incoming radiation and 34 units of outgoing radiation** **absorbed by it) back to space.** Thus, 65 units of solar radiation entering the atmosphere are reflected back into space. This account of incoming and outgoing radiation always maintains the balance of heat on the surface of the Earth.
**Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Distribution of Continents and Oceans
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - The interior of the continents receives greater amounts of rainfall than coastal areas of the world.
II. 	 The equatorial regions of the world receive precipitation throughout the year.
III. 	 In the northern hemisphere, the rainfall is more over the oceans than on the landmasses.
IV. 	 Between the latitudes 350 and 400 N and S of the equator, the rain is heavier on the eastern coasts and decreases towards the west.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The spatial distribution of precipitation is not uniform all over the world. The average annual precipitation for the world as a whole is about 97.5 centimeters but the land receives a lesser amount or rainfall than the oceans.
● In general, as we proceed from the equator towards the poles, rainfall goes on decreasing steadily. The **coastal areas of the world receive greater amounts of rainfall than the interior of the** **continents**. In the northern hemisphere, **the rainfall is more over the oceans than on the** **landmasses** of the world because of being great sources of water. **So, statement I is not correct** **and statement III is correct.**
● The **equatorial regions** and the western parts of temperate lands **receive precipitation** **throughout the year.** The former receive convectional type of rain while the later gets cyclonic cum orographic type through westerlies. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Between the latitudes 35****0** **and 40****0** **N and S of the equator, the rain is heavier on the eastern** **coasts and decreases towards the west.** But, between 450 and 650 N and S of the equator, due to the westerlies, the rainfall is first received on the western margins of the continents and it goes on decreasing towards the east. Wherever mountains run parallel to the coast, the rain is greater on the coastal plain, on the windward side and it decreases towards the leeward side. **So, statement IV is** **correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Clear sky
II. 	 Calm air
III. 	 High relative humidity
IV. 	 Long and cold nights
V. 	 Dew point below freezing point
How many of the above are ideal conditions for the formation of dew?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Evaporation** is a process by which water is transformed from liquid to gaseous state. Heat is the main cause for evaporation. The transformation of water vapour into water is called **condensation.** Condensation is caused by the loss of heat. When moist air is cooled, it may reach a level when its capacity to hold water vapour ceases. After condensation the water vapour or the moisture in the atmosphere takes one of the following forms — **dew, frost, fog and cloud.**
● **Dew** is formed when the moisture is deposited in the form of water droplets on cooler surfaces of solid objects (rather than nuclei in air above the surface) such as stones, grass blades and plant leaves, it is known as dew.
● The **ideal conditions for its formation are clear sky, calm air, high relative humidity and cold** **and long nights.** **So, points I, II, III and IV are correct.**
● For the formation of dew, it is necessary that the **dew point is above the freezing point. So, point** **V is not correct.** **So, only four of the above are ideal conditions for the formation of dew.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Frost** forms on cold surfaces when condensation takes place below freezing point, i.e. the dew point is at or below the freezing point. The ideal conditions for the formation of white frost are the same as those for the formation of dew, **except that the air** **temperature must be at or below the freezing point.**
● **Fog and Mist:** When the temperature of an air mass containing a large quantity of water vapour falls all of a sudden, condensation takes place within itself on fine dust particles. So, the fog is a cloud with its base at or very near to the ground. Because of the fog and mist, the visibility becomes poor to zero. In urban and industrial centres smoke provides plenty of nuclei which help the formation of fog and mist.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** The phenomenon of inversion of temperature is observed in intermontane valleys during winters.
**Statement II:** During winters, the mountain slopes cool very rapidly due to the quick radiation of heat.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is not correct
options.d: Statement I is not correct but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Inversion of Temperature:**
● **Long winter nights, clear sky, dry air** and **absence of winds** lead to quick radiation of heat from the Earth’s surface, as well as from the lower layers of the atmosphere. This results in the cooling of the air near the Earth’s surface. The upper layers which lose their heat not so quickly are comparatively warm. Hence, the normal condition in which temperature decreases with increasing height is reversed. The cooler air is nearer the Earth and the warmer air is aloft. In other words, **temperature increases** **with increasing height temporarily or locally.** This phenomenon is termed as **inversion of** **temperature**. Sometimes the cold and dense air remains near the surface for a number of days. So the phenomenon of **inversion of temperature is also seen for days together.**
● The phenomenon of **inversion of temperature is especially observed in intermontane valleys** **because** during winters, the **mountain slopes cool very rapidly due to the quick radiation of** **heat.** The air resting above them also becomes cold and its density increases. Hence, it moves down the slopes and settles down in the valleys. This air pushes the comparatively warmer air of the valleys upwards and leads to the phenomenon of inversion of temperature. Sometimes the temperature falls below the freezing point in the valleys leading to the occurrence of frost. **So, statements I and II are** **correct.** **So, both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements regarding air masses:
I. - They can acquire their characteristics from the surface over which they remain stationary.
II. 	 Tropical air masses originate over the lower latitudes of both land and sea.
III. 	 They are not formed in the polar regions.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
An air mass is an extensive portion of the atmosphere having uniform characteristics of temperature, pressure and moisture which are relatively homogeneous horizontally.
● An **air mass** develops when the air over a vast and **relatively uniform land or ocean surface** **remains stationary** for a long time to acquire the **temperature or moisture from** the surface. When the air remains over a homogenous area for a sufficiently longer time, it **acquires the** **characteristics of the area**. The homogenous regions can be the vast ocean surface or vast plains. The air with distinctive characteristics in terms of **temperature and humidity is called an airmass.** It is defined as a large body of air having little horizontal variation in temperature and moisture. **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● Each of the two divisions are then divided based upon the temperature of the surface over which they originate.
○ Arctic air masses, designated by the letter "A", originate over the Arctic or Antarctic regions and therefore are very cold. ○ **Polar air masses,** designated by the letter "P", originate over the higher latitudes of both land and sea and are therefore not as **cold as Arctic air masses. So, statement III is not** **correct.** ○ **Tropical air masses,** designated by the letter "T", **originate over the lower latitudes of** **both land and sea** and therefore are **warm/hot. So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.** **Subject:** Geography | Climate, Weather and Atmosphere **Tags:** Factual, Analytical

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference to jet streams:
I. - They are relatively narrow bands of strong wind in the upper levels of the atmosphere.
II.	 They generally blow from west to east all across the globe.
III.	 They are strongest during both the northern and southern hemisphere winters.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Jet streams** are relatively **narrow bands of strong wind** in the **upper levels of the atmosphere,** typically occurring around 30,000 feet (9,100 meters) in elevation. **So, statement I is correct.**
● Within jet streams, **the winds blow from west to east**, but the band often shifts north and south because jet streams follow the boundaries between hot and cold air. **So, statement II is correct.**
● Since these hot and cold air boundaries are most pronounced in winter, **jet streams are the strongest** **during both the northern and southern hemisphere winters. So, statement III is correct.**
● Jet streams can transport aerosols such as smoke and gases such as ozone, very long distances. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Interior of Earth and Continental Theories
questions.questionText:
With reference to tropical cyclones, consider the following characteristics:
I. - They lack well-defined warm and cold fronts.
II. 	 They are driven primarily by convectional currents.
III. 	 They are typically accompanied by strong anticyclones.
IV. 	 They are usually shallow with weak wind velocity as compared to temperate cyclones.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Typical cyclones** are an elliptical arrangement of isobars having **low pressure at the centre** with a convergence of winds within them. The wind direction in the cyclones is anticlockwise in the northern hemisphere and clockwise in the southern hemisphere.
● Tropical cyclones are formed along the zone of confluence of north-east and south-east trade winds. This zone is known as the Inter Tropical Convergence Zone (ITCZ). Cyclones generally occur in Mexico, the Southwestern and North Pacific Ocean, North Indian Ocean and South Pacific Ocean. **These** **cyclones differ from temperate cyclones in many ways:**
○ There are **no clear warm and cold fronts** as temperature seldom differs in the Inter Tropical Convergence Zone. **So, point I is correct.** ○ They **do not have a well-defined pattern of winds** and are **energised by convectional** **currents within them.** Generally, these are **shallow depressions and the velocity of** **winds is weak**. **So, point II and IV is correct.** ○ These **are not accompanied by anticyclones.** **So, point III is not correct.**
● The arrangement of isobars is almost circular. These are not extensive and have diametres of 160-640 km. However, a few of them become very violent and destroy the regions of their influence. They are called hurricanes in the Caribbean Sea, typhoons in China, Japan and the Philippines. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Water in the Atmosphere (Clouds, Condensation, Precipitation, Rainfall, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following types of rainfall is
formed where air rises and cools because of a
physical barrier?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Orographic or Relief Rainfall:**
● It is formed **where air rises and cools because of a topographic barrier.** When their temperature falls below the dew point, clouds are formed. These clouds cause widespread rain on the windward slopes of the mountain range. This type of rain is called orographic rainfall. However, when these winds cross over the mountain range and descend along the leeward slopes, they get warm and cause little rain. The region lying on the leeward side of the mountain receiving little rain is called a rainshadow area. A famous example of orographic rainfall is **Cherrapunji on the southern margin of the Khasi Hills** **in Meghalaya India.**
**Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Convectional Rain:** The air on being heated, becomes light and rises up in convection currents. As it rises, it expands and loses heat and consequently, condensation takes place and cumulous clouds are formed. With thunder and lightning, heavy rainfall takes place but this does not last long. Such rain is common in the summer or in the hotter part of the day. It is very common in the equatorial regions and interior parts of the continents, particularly in the northern hemisphere.
● **Convergence or Cyclonic Rainfall:** Convergence rainfall, produced where air currents converge and rise. In tropical regions where opposing air currents have comparable temperatures, the lifting is more or less vertical and is usually accompanied by convention.
● **Frontal rainfall:** When two large air masses of different densities and temperature meet, the warmer, moist air mass is lifted above the colder one. When this happens, the rising warm air mass condenses to form clouds, which cause extensive downpours. This rainfall is associated with thunder and lightning.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Distribution of Continents and Oceans
questions.questionText:
Which of the following is/are the cold ocean
currents in the Pacific Ocean?
I. - Oyashio Current
II. 	 Labrador Current
III. 	 Agulhas Current
IV. 	 Gulf Stream
Select the correct answer using the code given
below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The ocean currents are the large masses of surface water that circulate in regular patterns around the oceans. They can be classified into warm currents and cold currents depending upon their temperature.
● **Warm currents:** These currents flow from equatorial regions towards the Polar Regions and hence have a higher surface temperature. These currents flow in the clockwise direction in the northern hemisphere and in the anti-clockwise direction in the southern hemisphere.
● **Cold currents:** These currents flow from Polar Regions towards the equator and have a lower surface temperature. Cold ocean currents flow in the anti-clockwise direction in the northern hemisphere and in the clockwise direction in the southern hemisphere. **Distribution of Ocean Currents:**
● **Currents in the Pacific Ocean:** ○ **Warm Currents are;** North Equatorial Current, South Equatorial Current, Counter Equatorial Current, Kuroshio System, East Australia Current, and North Pacific Drift. ○ **Cold Currents are; Oyashio Current,** California Current, and Peruvian or Humboldt Current. **So, point I is correct.**
● **Currents in the Atlantic Ocean:** ○ **Warm currents are;** North Equatorial Current, South Equatorial Current, Equatorial Counter Current, **Gulf Stream**, Florida Current, and Brazilian Current. **So, point IV is not correct.** ○ **Cold Currents are;** Canaries Current, **Labrador Current**, Falkland Current, South Atlantic Drift, and Benguela Current. **So, point II is not correct.**
● **Currents in the Indian Ocean:** ○ **Drifts are;** The North East Monsoon Drift, and The South West Monsoon Drift. ○ **Warm currents are;** North Equatorial Current, South Equatorial Current, Mozambique Current, Madagascar Current and **Agulhas Current.
